prompts: A coach lists Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment, and Sprint Retrospective and asks which item does not belong with the others. Which is the odd one out?

correct.why: The Retrospective is a Scrum event; the other three are Scrum artifacts.

distractors.trap: The Product Backlog is an artifact, so it belongs with the group.
The Sprint Backlog is an artifact, so it belongs with the group.
The Increment is an artifact, so it belongs with the group.

explanation: Artifacts are the Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, and Increment; the Retrospective is an event, making it the odd one out.

prompts: An analyst measures the elapsed time from the moment work actually starts on an item until it is done, separate from the longer span counted from when the customer first requested it. Which metric counts only from work start?

correct.why: Cycle time measures elapsed time after work starts until completion.

distractors.trap: Lead time usually begins earlier, from request or commitment, so it spans more than cycle time.
Throughput counts how many items complete in a period, not the duration of a single item.
Velocity is story points per iteration, not the elapsed time of one item.

explanation: Cycle time runs from work start to completion; lead time begins earlier at request or commitment.

prompts: A Kanban team wants to monitor how work moves through its system. Select the two measures that are flow metrics describing movement of work, rather than earned value or baseline variance.

correct.why: Cycle time is a flow metric describing how long an item takes to move through the process.
Throughput is a flow metric counting how many items complete over a period.

distractors.trap: Schedule variance is an earned value measure against a baseline, not a flow metric.
The cost performance index is an earned value efficiency ratio, not a measure of work flow.
Planned value is an earned value baseline figure, not a flow metric.

explanation: Cycle time and throughput describe flow of work; the others are earned value constructs tied to baselines.

prompts: A team wants a chart that shows how many items are sitting in each workflow state over time so a widening band reveals where work piles up. Which chart should they use?

correct.why: A cumulative flow diagram shows work accumulating across states over time, making bottlenecks visible as widening bands.

distractors.trap: A burndown shows remaining work against time, not the distribution of work across states.
A velocity chart shows points completed per iteration, not work piling up by state.
A mood chart tracks team sentiment, not the flow of work items across states.

explanation: The cumulative flow diagram exposes bottlenecks by showing work accumulating in each state over time.

prompts: A team sizes backlog items by having each member privately pick a relative estimate and then reveal it simultaneously to discuss differences and converge. Which technique is this?

correct.why: Planning poker is the structured group estimation technique where members reveal relative estimates together and converge through discussion.

distractors.text: Expert judgment from a single specialist
distractors.trap: Expert judgment may come from one specialist without the simultaneous group reveal that defines planning poker.
Three-point estimating combines optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely values; it is not a simultaneous reveal technique.
Analogous estimating draws on data from a past similar effort, not a collaborative simultaneous reveal.

explanation: Simultaneous reveal of relative estimates to converge through discussion is planning poker.

prompts: Select the two Scrum events that occur at the end of a Sprint to inspect and adapt, one focused on the product and one on the process.

correct.why: The Sprint review happens at the end of the Sprint to inspect the increment and gather feedback.
The Sprint retrospective happens at the end of the Sprint to inspect and improve how the team works.

distractors.trap: Sprint planning starts the Sprint by selecting work; it is not an end-of-Sprint inspection.
The Daily Scrum is a daily synchronization, not an end-of-Sprint event.
Refinement is an ongoing preparation activity, not an end-of-Sprint inspection event.

explanation: At the end of a Sprint the review inspects the product and the retrospective inspects the process.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Cost Variance and the Cost Performance Index use the same two inputs. What is the difference between them?

correct.text: Cost Variance is a difference (EV minus AC) in currency; CPI is a ratio (EV divided by AC) with no units
correct.why: Both compare earned value to actual cost, but a variance reports the gap in money while an index reports relative efficiency.

distractors.text: Cost Variance uses Planned Value while CPI uses Actual Cost
distractors.trap: Both metrics use Actual Cost, not Planned Value; that is the schedule side.

distractors.text: Cost Variance forecasts the final cost while CPI measures only the past
distractors.trap: Neither is a forecast on its own; EAC and ETC are the forecasting metrics.

distractors.text: They are interchangeable and always carry the same sign and meaning
distractors.trap: A negative CV corresponds to a CPI below 1, but one is money and the other is a unitless ratio - they are not the same number.

explanation: Same inputs, different math: subtract for the dollar gap (CV), divide for the efficiency ratio (CPI).

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A status report shows CPI of 0.9. Taken on its own, what does that tell the project manager?

correct.text: For every dollar spent the project is earning about 90 cents of value, so it is over budget and cost-inefficient
correct.why: A CPI below 1 means earned value trails actual cost; the work is costing more than it is returning in budgeted value.

distractors.text: The project is behind schedule by roughly 10 percent
distractors.trap: Schedule status comes from SPI, not CPI; a cost index says nothing directly about time.

distractors.text: The project is under budget and running efficiently
distractors.trap: Under budget would be CPI above 1; 0.9 is below the break-even point of 1.0.

distractors.text: The project will finish exactly at the Budget at Completion
distractors.trap: A CPI below 1 signals a likely overrun, not an on-budget finish; finishing at BAC implies CPI near 1 going forward.

explanation: Read any performance index against 1.0: above 1 is good, below 1 is unfavorable. CPI is the cost lens, so 0.9 means cost overrun.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A weekly report lists Schedule Variance as a negative number. Ignoring cost, what does a negative SV indicate?

correct.text: The project has earned less value than was planned for this point, so it is behind schedule
correct.why: SV equals EV minus PV; a negative result means completed value lags the planned value and the work is behind.

distractors.text: The project has overspent its budget for the work done so far
distractors.trap: Overspending is a negative Cost Variance; SV measures schedule progress, not money.

distractors.text: The project is ahead of schedule because the value is negative
distractors.trap: Negative is unfavorable for variance; ahead of schedule would be a positive SV.

distractors.text: The Planned Value was set too high and the baseline must be wrong
distractors.trap: A negative SV reflects performance against the baseline, not proof that the baseline itself is flawed.

explanation: For both variances, negative is unfavorable. SV negative means earned value is below planned value - behind schedule.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Budget at Completion and Estimate at Completion are often mixed up. Which statement correctly contrasts them?

correct.text: Budget at Completion is the approved total planned budget; Estimate at Completion is the current forecast of what the total will actually cost
correct.why: BAC is fixed at the baseline; EAC is recalculated from performance to predict the real final cost.

distractors.text: Budget at Completion is the forecast final cost; Estimate at Completion is the original approved budget
distractors.trap: Reversed - BAC is the original approved figure and EAC is the live forecast.

distractors.text: Both are forecasts that change every reporting period as performance shifts
distractors.trap: BAC is a fixed baseline that changes only through formal change control; only EAC moves with performance.

distractors.text: Budget at Completion is the cost of remaining work; Estimate at Completion is the cost already spent
distractors.trap: Remaining work is ETC and spent-to-date is AC; neither defines BAC or EAC.

explanation: BAC is the plan (approved total). EAC is the prediction (where you now expect to land). Compare them with VAC equals BAC minus EAC.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Variance at Completion is a forecast metric. What does VAC actually express?

correct.text: The expected overrun or underrun at the end, found as Budget at Completion minus Estimate at Completion
correct.why: VAC compares the approved total budget to the forecast total cost, so a positive VAC predicts an underrun.

distractors.text: The difference between Earned Value and Actual Cost at the current moment
distractors.trap: That is Cost Variance, a point-in-time measure; VAC is a projection to the end of the project.

distractors.text: The cost of the work still remaining to be done
distractors.trap: Remaining-work cost is ETC; VAC is the projected budget gap at completion, not the remaining spend.

distractors.text: The efficiency the remaining work must achieve to finish on budget
distractors.trap: That required efficiency is TCPI; VAC is a money gap, not a target ratio.

explanation: VAC equals BAC minus EAC - the forecast of how far over or under budget the project will end up. Positive means under budget.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Which of these is NOT a forecasting or to-complete metric in earned value analysis?

correct.why: Planned Value is a baseline measurement of scheduled work, not a forecast of future or remaining outcomes.

distractors.trap: EAC is a forecast of the total final cost, so it belongs to the forecasting family.
ETC forecasts the cost of the remaining work, so it is a forward-looking metric.
TCPI is the efficiency the remaining work must achieve, a to-complete forecasting measure.

explanation: EAC, ETC, and TCPI all look forward. Planned Value is a baseline input (budget for scheduled work), not a forecast.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: The cost management plan and the cost baseline are different kinds of artifacts. Which description matches each?

correct.text: The cost management plan describes how costs will be planned and controlled; the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used to measure performance
correct.why: The plan sets the rules and process; the baseline is the numeric reference those rules are applied against.

distractors.text: The cost management plan is the approved budget; the cost baseline describes how costs will be controlled
distractors.trap: Reversed - the baseline is the numeric budget and the plan is the how-to document.

distractors.text: Both are numeric budgets, just at different levels of detail
distractors.trap: Only the baseline is a budget; the management plan is a process document, not a set of figures.

distractors.text: The cost management plan is produced during Control Costs and the baseline during Estimate Costs
distractors.trap: The plan comes from Plan Cost Management and the baseline from Determine Budget; neither originates in Control Costs or Estimate Costs.

explanation: Plan equals the governance and method; baseline equals the approved time-phased number you compare actuals against.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Estimate Costs and Determine Budget are sequential cost processes. Which statement correctly distinguishes the two?

correct.text: Estimate Costs develops the cost of individual activities; Determine Budget aggregates those approved estimates into the cost baseline
correct.why: Estimating produces the building-block numbers; budgeting sums them (with reserves) into the authorized time-phased baseline.

distractors.text: Determine Budget estimates individual activities; Estimate Costs rolls them up into the baseline
distractors.trap: Reversed - estimating comes first at the activity level, budgeting aggregates afterward.

distractors.text: Estimate Costs monitors spending while Determine Budget approves changes to the baseline
distractors.trap: Monitoring spending and managing baseline changes is Control Costs, not either of these planning processes.

distractors.text: Both processes only occur once the project is already in execution
distractors.trap: Both are planning processes that establish the baseline before execution, not execution-phase activities.

explanation: Estimate Costs equals price the pieces; Determine Budget equals add them up plus reserves to set the baseline.

eco_task: Support organizational change and assess business value
prompts: Two projects are being compared for selection. One has a Net Present Value and the other an Internal Rate of Return. What does each figure represent?

correct.text: Net Present Value is the value of the project in today's money at a chosen discount rate; Internal Rate of Return is the discount rate at which that value would be zero
correct.why: NPV is a money amount given a rate; IRR is the break-even rate where NPV equals zero.

distractors.text: Net Present Value is the break-even discount rate; Internal Rate of Return is the value in today's money
distractors.trap: Reversed - NPV is a monetary amount and IRR is the rate that drives NPV to zero.

distractors.text: Both express the project return as a single percentage for easy ranking
distractors.trap: Only IRR is a percentage; NPV is a currency amount, not a rate.

distractors.text: Net Present Value ignores the time value of money while Internal Rate of Return accounts for it
distractors.trap: NPV is built entirely on discounting future cash flows, so it very much accounts for the time value of money.

explanation: NPV equals dollars-today at a given rate; IRR equals the rate that makes those dollars net to zero. Both rest on discounting.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A chart of accounts and a code of accounts have similar names but serve different purposes. Which statement is correct?

correct.text: A chart of accounts organizes financial cost categories; a code of accounts uniquely identifies components of the work breakdown structure
correct.why: The chart is an accounting/financial coding scheme, while the code of accounts labels WBS elements.

distractors.text: A code of accounts organizes financial categories; a chart of accounts identifies WBS components
distractors.trap: Reversed - the code of accounts maps to the WBS and the chart of accounts to financial categories.

distractors.text: Both are numbering systems for the work breakdown structure
distractors.trap: Only the code of accounts maps to the WBS; the chart of accounts is a financial monitoring scheme.

distractors.text: Both are accounting systems with no connection to project scope
distractors.trap: The code of accounts is tied directly to the WBS scope structure, not purely to accounting.

explanation: Chart equals financial categories (accounting); code equals WBS component identifiers (scope). Similar names, different jobs.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A control account differs from a work package in its role within earned value management. Which statement is correct?

correct.text: A control account is a higher-level management point where scope, schedule, budget, and actual cost are integrated and compared to earned value
correct.why: Performance is measured at the control account, which integrates several work elements above the work-package level.

distractors.text: A control account is the lowest level of planned work in the WBS
distractors.trap: The lowest level of planned work is the work package; the control account sits above it.

distractors.text: A control account is a financial coding scheme unrelated to the WBS
distractors.trap: That describes a chart of accounts; a control account is a WBS management point for performance measurement.

distractors.text: A control account is the reserve held outside the cost baseline
distractors.trap: Reserve outside the baseline is management reserve; a control account is a measurement point inside the WBS.

explanation: Control account equals the integration and measurement point above work packages; the work package equals the detailed lowest level of planned work.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Earned value analysis and earned value management are related terms. Which statement captures their relationship?

correct.text: Earned value management is the broader system and governance that produce the data; earned value analysis is the act of assessing performance using that data
correct.why: EVM is the integrated methodology and processes; EVA is the analysis performed on the measures it generates.

distractors.text: Earned value analysis is the broad governance system; earned value management is the single calculation step
distractors.trap: Reversed - management is the broad system, analysis is the assessment activity.

distractors.text: They are unrelated; one is a cost concept and the other a schedule concept
distractors.trap: Both integrate scope, schedule, and cost; they are not split along a single dimension.

distractors.text: Earned value analysis replaces earned value management in adaptive projects
distractors.trap: One does not replace the other; analysis is part of using the management system, in any approach that applies it.

explanation: EVM equals the system that integrates scope, schedule, and cost; EVA equals the analysis you run on its outputs.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Select the TWO statements that are true about how reserves relate to the cost baseline.

correct.text: Contingency reserve is included within the cost baseline
correct.why: Contingency reserve funds identified risks and is part of the time-phased cost baseline.

correct.text: Management reserve is held outside the cost baseline
correct.why: Management reserve covers unforeseen in-scope work and sits above the baseline in the total project budget.

distractors.text: Management reserve is included within the cost baseline
distractors.trap: Management reserve is deliberately excluded from the baseline; it lives in the project budget above it.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve is held outside the cost baseline
distractors.trap: Contingency reserve is inside the baseline; it is management reserve that is outside.

distractors.text: Neither reserve has any connection to the cost baseline
distractors.trap: Both relate to it - one is inside the baseline and one sits just above it.

explanation: Inside the baseline equals contingency reserve (known risks). Outside the baseline equals management reserve (unknown-unknowns). Together with the work budget they form the project budget.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Reserve analysis is sometimes confused with the reserves themselves. What is reserve analysis?

correct.text: An analytical technique used to determine how much contingency and management reserve a project needs
correct.why: Reserve analysis is the method for sizing reserves; the reserves are its output, not the technique.

distractors.text: The pool of money set aside for identified risks within the baseline
distractors.trap: That is contingency reserve, an output of the analysis, not the analytical technique itself.

distractors.text: The process of monitoring spending against the cost baseline
distractors.trap: Monitoring spend against the baseline is Control Costs, not reserve analysis.

distractors.text: The forecast of the total final cost of the project
distractors.trap: The forecast of total final cost is EAC; reserve analysis sizes reserves, it does not forecast completion cost.

explanation: Reserve analysis is the technique; contingency and management reserves are what it determines.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Control Costs and Determine Budget are different cost processes. Which statement correctly assigns their purposes?

correct.text: Determine Budget establishes the approved cost baseline; Control Costs monitors performance against that baseline and manages changes to it
correct.why: Budgeting sets the baseline up front; controlling watches actuals against it and routes changes through change control.

distractors.text: Control Costs establishes the baseline; Determine Budget monitors performance against it
distractors.trap: Reversed - the baseline is set in Determine Budget and monitored in Control Costs.

distractors.text: Both processes establish the baseline at different points in planning
distractors.trap: Only Determine Budget sets the baseline; Control Costs is a monitoring and controlling process.

distractors.text: Both processes monitor actual spending against the baseline
distractors.trap: Determine Budget creates the baseline; it does not monitor actuals - that is Control Costs alone.

explanation: Determine Budget equals build the baseline; Control Costs equals watch actuals against it and manage changes.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: An estimate and a budget are not the same thing. Which statement captures the difference?

correct.text: An estimate forecasts the probable cost of work; a budget is the approved amount authorized for that work
correct.why: Estimating predicts likely cost, while budgeting is the formally approved authorization to spend.

distractors.text: An estimate is the approved authorization; a budget is the rough prediction
distractors.trap: Reversed - the approved authorization is the budget and the prediction is the estimate.

distractors.text: Both are approved figures that cannot change without change control
distractors.trap: An estimate is a prediction that can be refined freely; only the approved budget baseline needs change control to alter.

distractors.text: An estimate always equals the final budget once approved
distractors.trap: Budgets often add reserves and adjustments on top of estimates; they are not automatically equal.

explanation: Estimate equals likely cost (a forecast); budget equals the approved, authorized amount. Estimates feed budgets but are not the same.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A cost baseline is described as time-phased. What does time-phased mean in this context?

correct.text: The approved budget is distributed across the schedule so spending is expected period by period, forming the planned-value curve
correct.why: A time-phased baseline maps budget to when the work is scheduled, which is exactly how Planned Value is generated over time.

distractors.text: The entire budget is available on day one and drawn down freely
distractors.trap: Time-phasing spreads the budget across the schedule; it is not a single lump available immediately.

distractors.text: The baseline is recalculated each period from actual costs incurred
distractors.trap: Recalculating from actuals describes forecasting (EAC), not the fixed time-phased baseline.

distractors.text: The budget is split only between labor and materials with no schedule dimension
distractors.trap: Time-phasing is about distribution over time, not a split by cost category.

explanation: Time-phased equals budget mapped to the schedule over time - the source of the Planned Value (PV) curve used in EVM.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Project funding requirements and the cost baseline serve related but distinct roles. Which statement is correct?

correct.text: The cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget for the work; funding requirements describe the financing needed, by period and source, to support that spending
correct.why: The baseline is the spend plan; funding requirements ensure the money is available when the baseline says it will be needed, often in stepped increments.

distractors.text: Funding requirements are the approved spend plan; the cost baseline describes when financing arrives
distractors.trap: Reversed - the baseline is the spend plan and funding requirements describe the financing timing.

distractors.text: They are identical because both track money over time
distractors.trap: The baseline measures cost performance; funding requirements address cash availability - related but not the same.

distractors.text: Funding requirements exclude reserves while the cost baseline includes management reserve
distractors.trap: The cost baseline excludes management reserve; funding requirements typically reflect the total need including reserves.

explanation: Cost baseline equals the time-phased spend plan; funding requirements equal the money that must be available, by period and source, to fund it.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Which one of these is NOT one of the three independent variables measured directly in earned value management?

correct.why: CPI is a derived ratio calculated from earned value and actual cost, not one of the three directly measured variables.

distractors.trap: Planned Value is one of the three core measured quantities - the budget for scheduled work.
Earned Value is a core measured quantity - the budgeted value of completed work.
Actual Cost is a core measured quantity - the real money spent on the work.

explanation: The three primary EVM measures are PV, EV, and AC. CPI, CV, SV, and SPI are all derived from them.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A status report shows SPI of 1.1 and CPI of 0.95. What is the combined picture?

correct.text: Ahead of schedule but over budget - earning value faster than planned while spending more than the work is worth
correct.why: SPI above 1 means schedule progress beats plan; CPI below 1 means cost efficiency is unfavorable.

distractors.trap: Inverted both - SPI above 1 is ahead of schedule and CPI below 1 is over budget.

distractors.text: Ahead of schedule and under budget, so no concerns
distractors.trap: CPI of 0.95 is below 1, meaning over budget; the cost side is a concern.

distractors.text: On schedule and on budget because the two indices roughly average to 1
distractors.trap: Indices are not averaged; each is read separately against 1.0, and here they point in opposite directions.

explanation: Read each index against 1.0 independently. SPI 1.1 equals ahead of schedule; CPI 0.95 equals over budget. Speed gained, money lost.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A buffer and a contingency reserve both absorb variability, but they are not the same idea. Which statement is correct?

correct.text: A buffer is a protective allowance embedded in timing or flow; a contingency reserve is budget or schedule capacity set aside specifically for identified risks
correct.why: Buffers protect delivery against general variability, while contingency reserves are tied to specific identified risks with responses.

distractors.text: A contingency reserve protects flow against general variability; a buffer is set aside for identified risks
distractors.trap: Reversed - it is the buffer that absorbs general variability and the contingency reserve that targets identified risks.

distractors.text: Both are reserves held outside the cost baseline for unknown-unknowns
distractors.trap: Unknown-unknowns are funded by management reserve; contingency reserve sits inside the baseline.

distractors.text: They are identical and the terms are fully interchangeable
distractors.trap: They overlap but differ - a buffer is a flow or timing allowance, a contingency reserve is risk-specific capacity.

explanation: Buffer equals a flow or timing cushion against general variability; contingency reserve equals capacity set aside for specific identified risks.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Life-cycle costing takes a wider view than the project cost baseline. What does life-cycle costing evaluate?

correct.text: All costs of the deliverable across its full life - acquisition, operation, and maintenance - not just the cost of the project that creates it
correct.why: Life-cycle costing spans the whole useful life, while the cost baseline only controls the project-phase spend.

distractors.text: Only the time-phased project spend used to measure cost performance
distractors.trap: That is the cost baseline; life-cycle costing reaches beyond the project into operation and disposal.

distractors.text: Only the ongoing operating cost after the project ends
distractors.trap: Operating cost is one slice; life-cycle costing sums acquisition, operation, and end-of-life costs together.

distractors.text: Only the financing and cash-flow timing during the project
distractors.trap: Financing timing is a funding concern; life-cycle costing is about total cost across the whole life, not cash timing.

explanation: Life-cycle costing equals total cost from cradle to grave (acquisition plus operation plus disposal). The cost baseline is only the project-build slice.

eco_task: Identify who authorizes the project charter
prompts: Who formally issues and signs the project charter, the act that authorizes the project to exist and grants the project manager authority to apply organizational resources?

correct.why: The charter is issued by the sponsor or initiating authority because that person sits high enough to commit organizational resources to the project.

distractors.trap: The project manager is empowered by the charter and may help draft it, but does not authorize it; that would be self-authorization.
A PMO may support or template the charter but is not the authority that funds and sanctions the project.
The customer receives and uses the result but does not typically issue the internal charter that commits the performing organization.

explanation: The sponsor, an authority external to the project with funding power, issues and signs the charter to authorize the project and the project manager.

eco_task: Contrast configuration management and change control
prompts: A team must keep the approved descriptions, versions, and physical and functional characteristics of product components correct and consistent over time. Which discipline owns that, as opposed to the one that evaluates and approves proposed changes?

correct.why: Configuration management governs the identification and integrity of configuration items and their approved states, ensuring descriptions remain correct and consistent.

distractors.trap: Change control evaluates, approves, or rejects proposed modifications; it does not by itself maintain the integrity of item descriptions and versions.
Quality control inspects deliverables against requirements; it is not the discipline that maintains configuration item integrity.
Integrated change control coordinates decisions about changes across the project, again the change side rather than configuration integrity.

explanation: Configuration management keeps item descriptions and versions correct and consistent; change control is the separate process of assessing and approving proposed changes.

eco_task: Relate change request and approving body
prompts: A change request is the formal proposal to modify a baseline. The formally constituted group that reviews it and approves, defers, or rejects it is the which of the following?

correct.why: The Change Control Board is the body that reviews change requests and records the decision to approve, defer, or reject them.

distractors.trap: The change log records the status of changes over time; it is a document, not the deciding body.
The configuration management system controls item versions and integrity, not the approval of change requests.
A steering committee gives broad governance direction, but the named body that decides on project change requests is the Change Control Board.

explanation: Change request equals the proposal; Change Control Board equals the body that decides; change log equals the record of decisions.

prompts: At the end of a project phase, the governing body holds a formal review against defined criteria to decide whether the project should continue, change, or stop before the next phase begins. What is this review point called?

correct.why: A phase gate, also called a tollgate or stage gate, is a formal end-of-phase review where defined criteria determine whether to proceed, change, or stop.

distractors.trap: A milestone marks a significant point in time but carries no built-in approval criteria or proceed-or-stop decision.
A kickoff launches work and aligns the team; it is not an end-of-phase continuation decision.
A retrospective reflects on how to improve ways of working; it does not authorize continuation to the next phase.

explanation: A phase gate, tollgate, or stage gate is the governance decision point at a phase boundary that authorizes continuation, change, or termination.

prompts: Why is a phase gate not the same thing as a milestone?

correct.text: A gate is a governance decision point with approval criteria, while a milestone is simply a significant marker in the schedule
correct.why: Gates carry a proceed, change, or stop decision; milestones only flag that a notable point has been reached.

distractors.text: A gate has zero duration while a milestone always spans several weeks
distractors.trap: Milestones also have zero duration; duration is not the distinguishing feature.

distractors.text: A milestone authorizes continuation, while a gate only records progress
distractors.trap: Reversed: the gate authorizes continuation, and the milestone records the point reached.

distractors.text: They are interchangeable terms for the same scheduling event
distractors.trap: Synonym trap: a decision gate and a status marker serve different governance purposes.

explanation: Gate equals a decision point with criteria; milestone equals a significant scheduled marker with no inherent decision.

eco_task: Apply the go or no-go decision concept
prompts: At a gate review the governing body weighs the updated business case and current performance and formally determines whether the work should proceed, pause, or stop. What is this determination called?

correct.why: A go or no-go decision is the formal proceed-or-stop determination made against defined criteria, typically at a gate.

distractors.trap: The phase gate is the review point or mechanism; the go or no-go decision is the actual proceed-or-stop determination made there.
A lessons learned review captures insight for improvement, not an authorization to continue or terminate.
Variance analysis examines differences from baseline; it informs but is not itself the continuation decision.

explanation: The gate is where the decision is made; the go or no-go decision is the proceed, pause, or stop determination itself.

prompts: On a project the scope, schedule, and cost are defined as fully as possible early on, baselined, and then managed and controlled against those baselines throughout. Which life cycle is in use?

correct.why: A predictive life cycle fixes scope early, baselines it, and manages change against the baseline, which fits stable, well-understood work.

distractors.trap: An adaptive life cycle expects scope to evolve through iterations rather than being fully defined and baselined up front.
An incremental approach delivers in successive usable pieces rather than defining and baselining the whole scope first.
An iterative approach refines the solution through repeated cycles, not by locking scope to an early baseline.

explanation: Defining scope fully early and managing to the baseline is the signature of a predictive, plan-driven life cycle.

eco_task: Contrast adaptive and predictive life cycles
prompts: What is the core difference between an adaptive and a predictive life cycle?

correct.text: An adaptive life cycle expects scope to evolve and delivers through iterations, while a predictive life cycle defines scope fully early and manages to the baseline
correct.why: The contrast is how scope is treated: emergent and iterative versus fixed early and controlled against a baseline.

distractors.text: An adaptive life cycle forbids any change, while a predictive life cycle welcomes constant change
distractors.trap: Reversed: adaptive embraces evolving scope, while predictive controls change tightly against a baseline.

distractors.text: An adaptive life cycle has no customer involvement, while a predictive one requires daily customer input
distractors.trap: Adaptive approaches in fact rely on frequent customer feedback; this reverses the reality.

distractors.text: They differ only in the project documents used, not in how scope is handled
distractors.trap: The real difference is the handling of scope and change, not merely paperwork.

explanation: Adaptive equals emergent scope delivered iteratively; predictive equals early-fixed scope managed against a baseline.

prompts: A team repeatedly reworks and refines the same prototype, improving its accuracy each cycle, rather than shipping finished slices of usable functionality one at a time. This emphasis describes which approach?

correct.why: An iterative approach refines the same solution through repeated cycles to improve correctness, distinct from delivering it in finished pieces.

distractors.trap: An incremental approach delivers successive usable pieces of the final product, whereas iteration refines the same thing repeatedly.
A predictive approach plans and delivers against an early baseline, not through repeated refinement cycles.
A hybrid combines predictive and adaptive elements, but the described single emphasis on refinement is specifically iteration.

explanation: Iterative equals repeated refinement of the same solution to improve it; incremental equals delivering the solution in successive usable pieces.

prompts: A project runs the well-understood infrastructure build to a fixed plan and baseline, while the uncertain customer-facing features are delivered in short adaptive iterations. Which development approach best describes this combination?

correct.why: A hybrid approach deliberately combines predictive and adaptive elements so each part of the work uses the most fitting method.

distractors.trap: A purely predictive approach would manage all the work to a fixed baseline, not run part of it in adaptive iterations.
A purely adaptive approach would iterate across the whole effort, not fix the infrastructure work to a plan and baseline.
Incremental describes delivering in pieces, but it does not capture the deliberate mixing of predictive and adaptive methods here.

explanation: Mixing predictive and adaptive methods within one project to fit each part of the work is the defining feature of a hybrid approach.

prompts: How many performance domains does the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition define?

correct.why: PMBOK 8 organizes project performance into seven performance domains: Stakeholders, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Resources, Governance, and Risk.

distractors.trap: Eight was the number of performance domains in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, not the Eighth.
Five corresponds to the focus areas in PMBOK 8, not to the number of performance domains.
Ten echoes the old knowledge areas of earlier process-based editions, not the performance domains.

explanation: PMBOK 8 has seven performance domains; the common trap is the eight domains from PMBOK 7.

prompts: In the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition, what were the traditional Process Groups renamed to?

correct.why: PMBOK 8 renames the five Process Groups as Focus Areas while retaining the underlying initiating-through-closing structure.

distractors.trap: Performance domains are a separate construct in PMBOK 7 and 8; they are not the renamed Process Groups.
Knowledge areas were the topic-based groupings of earlier editions, not the new name for Process Groups.
Principles are the value-based statements that guide behavior, not a renaming of the Process Groups.

explanation: PMBOK 8 calls the five Process Groups Focus Areas; performance domains and principles are different constructs.

prompts: How many principles does the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition present?

correct.why: PMBOK 8 presents six principles that guide project management behavior and decision making.

distractors.trap: Twelve was the number of principles in the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, a classic carryover trap.
Seven matches the number of performance domains in PMBOK 8, not the principles.
Five matches the focus areas in PMBOK 8, not the count of principles.

explanation: PMBOK 8 has six principles; the trap is the twelve principles from PMBOK 7.

prompts: Three of the items below are PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition performance domains. Which one is NOT a PMBOK 8 performance domain?

correct.why: Team was a performance domain in PMBOK 7; in PMBOK 8 the equivalent area is named Resources, so Team is the odd one out.

distractors.trap: Governance is one of the seven PMBOK 8 performance domains, so it does belong in the set.
Finance is a PMBOK 8 performance domain, replacing the earlier cost-only emphasis, so it belongs.
Risk is one of the seven PMBOK 8 performance domains, so it is not the odd one out.

explanation: The seven PMBOK 8 performance domains are Stakeholders, Scope, Schedule, Finance, Resources, Governance, and Risk; Team is a PMBOK 7 label that does not appear.

prompts: Approximately how many processes does the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition organize project work into?

correct.why: PMBOK 8 returns to a process orientation with roughly forty processes arranged across the five focus areas.

distractors.trap: Forty-nine was the process count in the PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, not the Eighth.
Seven is the number of performance domains, not the number of processes.
Five is the number of focus areas, the renamed process groups, not the process count.

explanation: PMBOK 8 has approximately forty processes; the trap is the forty-nine from PMBOK 6.

prompts: Quality control has checked a deliverable against its requirements and confirmed it conforms, but the sponsor has not yet formally signed it off. At this point the deliverable is best described as which of the following?

correct.why: A validated deliverable has been verified against requirements but has not yet received the formal customer or sponsor approval that makes it accepted.

distractors.trap: Acceptance requires formal sign-off by the customer or sponsor, which has not happened yet in this scenario.
Work performance data is raw measurement from execution, not a checked-against-requirements deliverable.
A configuration item is an item under formal change control; the scenario is about verification status, not change discipline.

explanation: Validated means checked against requirements; accepted adds the formal approval. Verified but unsigned equals validated, not accepted.

prompts: A sponsor signs the document that formally permits work on the project to begin and commits resources to it. The specific act of formally allowing work to start is best termed which of the following?

correct.why: Authorization is the formal act of allowing work on a project, phase, or activity to begin, typically through the charter.

distractors.trap: Acceptance confirms a completed deliverable meets requirements; it is not the act of permitting work to start.
Validation assesses that a deliverable meets intended needs; it does not start the work.
Escalation raises a matter to higher authority; it is unrelated to authorizing the start of work.

explanation: Authorization is the formal green light to begin work, distinct from accepting or validating finished outputs.

eco_task: Differentiate gold plating from scope creep
prompts: Without any change request, a developer adds extra polish and features that the customer never asked for because the team thought it would be nice. What is this specifically called?

correct.why: Gold plating is unrequested extra work the team adds on its own initiative, beyond what the customer required.

distractors.trap: Scope creep is uncontrolled expansion driven by added or changed requirements, often from stakeholders, not unrequested team-added extras.
Progressive elaboration is the legitimate refining of scope detail over time, not adding unrequested features.
Change control is the governed process for evaluating changes; the scenario describes uncontrolled team-added extras instead.

explanation: Gold plating equals the team adding unrequested extras; scope creep equals uncontrolled growth of scope, usually stakeholder driven.

prompts: If delegation moves a task down to others to perform, escalation moves a decision or issue in which direction?

correct.text: Up to a higher level of authority for attention or resolution
correct.why: Escalation raises an issue or decision upward when it cannot be resolved at the current level, the mirror of delegating downward.

distractors.text: Sideways to a peer team with equal authority
distractors.trap: Escalation is specifically upward to higher authority, not a lateral transfer to a peer.
Moving work downward describes delegation, which is the opposite of escalation.

distractors.text: Outward to an external vendor under contract
distractors.trap: Outsourcing or procurement moves work to a vendor; it is not the same as escalating a decision upward.

explanation: Delegation moves tasks down; escalation moves decisions and issues up to higher authority.

prompts: During quantitative risk analysis a team multiplies each risk's probability by its monetary impact and combines the results to size a contingency reserve. Which technique are they applying?

correct.why: Expected monetary value combines probability and impact into an average expected outcome, commonly used to size contingency reserves.

distractors.trap: Monetary impact is only the cost or benefit if the event actually occurs; expected monetary value weights it by probability.
Net present value discounts future cash flows for investment decisions; it is not the probability-weighted risk calculation described.
Sensitivity analysis shows which variables most affect outcomes; it does not produce the probability-times-impact figure described.

explanation: Expected monetary value equals probability times impact summed across outcomes, distinct from the raw impact alone.

prompts: Under which contract type does the seller carry the greatest cost risk, absorbing any overrun beyond the agreed amount?

correct.why: The price is fixed regardless of the seller's actual cost, so the seller absorbs every overrun and carries the most cost risk.

distractors.trap: Here the buyer reimburses allowable costs, so the buyer bears most of the cost risk, not the seller.
Risk is shared because the buyer pays for actual labor and materials at agreed rates; the seller is not absorbing overruns.
Costs are reimbursed by the buyer and only the fee varies, so the buyer still carries the bulk of cost risk.

explanation: In a firm fixed price contract the seller commits to a set price and absorbs any cost overrun, placing maximum cost risk on the seller. Cost-reimbursable and T and M types shift more cost risk to the buyer.

prompts: Work must begin immediately to augment the team with a few specialists, but the scope cannot yet be fully defined and is expected to be small. Which contract type best fits this situation?

correct.why: T and M suits undefined or small scope; it pays agreed rates for actual labor and materials and often carries a not-to-exceed cap.

distractors.trap: A firm fixed price needs a well-defined scope so the price can be set; undefined scope makes it unworkable.
Award-fee arrangements are heavier instruments suited to larger efforts with subjective performance evaluation, not a quick small staff augmentation.

distractors.text: Fixed price with economic price adjustment (FP-EPA)
distractors.trap: FP-EPA is a fixed-price variant for long-duration buys exposed to inflation, still requiring a defined scope.

explanation: Time and materials is the hybrid of choice when scope is not yet defined and the effort is small, since it bills actual hours and materials at preset rates, frequently bounded by a not-to-exceed limit.

prompts: Three of the following contract types belong to the cost-reimbursable family. Which one does NOT belong with the others?

correct.why: FPIF is a fixed-price contract with an incentive formula; it is not cost-reimbursable, making it the odd one out.

distractors.trap: CPFF reimburses allowable costs plus a fixed fee, so it is squarely in the cost-reimbursable family.
CPIF reimburses costs and varies the fee by performance, a cost-reimbursable type.
CPAF reimburses costs plus a buyer-evaluated award fee, also cost-reimbursable.

explanation: CPFF, CPIF, and CPAF all reimburse the seller's allowable costs and differ only in how the fee is set. FPIF is a fixed-price contract, so it is the outlier.

prompts: A contract adjusts the seller's fee using a predefined formula tied to measurable cost and schedule targets. What is this fee arrangement called?

correct.why: An incentive fee is earned through a defined formula tied to objective, measurable performance targets such as cost or schedule.

distractors.trap: An award fee is set by the buyer's subjective evaluation against stated criteria, not a fixed formula on measured results.
A fixed fee stays constant and does not move with performance against targets.
A ceiling price is the maximum the buyer will pay, not a fee mechanism tied to performance.

explanation: An incentive fee changes according to a preset formula linked to objective performance measures. That formula-based, measurable nature is what separates it from a subjective award fee.

prompts: How does an award fee differ from an incentive fee in a contract?

correct.text: An award fee is determined by the buyer's subjective evaluation against stated criteria, whereas an incentive fee follows a predefined formula on measured results
correct.why: Award fees rest on a periodic buyer judgment of performance, while incentive fees are computed objectively from a formula tied to targets.

distractors.text: An award fee uses a fixed formula while an incentive fee is subjective
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; the incentive fee is the formula-based one and the award fee is the subjective one.

distractors.text: Both are identical mechanisms with different names
distractors.trap: They are distinct: one is subjectively judged and the other is formula-driven, so they are not interchangeable.

distractors.text: An award fee is paid only on fixed-price contracts and an incentive fee only on cost contracts
distractors.trap: Both fee styles appear across contract families; this distinction is incorrect.

explanation: The defining difference is how the fee is decided: an award fee depends on the buyer's subjective performance evaluation against criteria, while an incentive fee is calculated from a predefined formula tied to objective targets.

prompts: On a fixed price incentive fee contract, costs rise to the level at which the seller becomes responsible for every additional dollar of cost. What is this specific cost level called?

correct.why: The point of total assumption is the cost above which the seller bears 100 percent of any further cost overrun.

distractors.trap: The ceiling price is the maximum the buyer will pay; it is a price, not the cost level where the seller starts absorbing all overrun.
The target cost is the planned cost basis used for incentive sharing, reached well before the seller assumes all overrun.
The share ratio describes how cost variances are split between buyer and seller, not the cost level itself.

explanation: The point of total assumption (PTA) on an FPIF contract is the cost figure beyond which the seller absorbs all additional costs up to the ceiling price. It is distinct from the ceiling price, which is a maximum payment.

prompts: In a fixed price incentive fee contract, which value sets the maximum amount the buyer is ever obligated to pay?

correct.why: The ceiling price is the contractual cap on what the buyer will pay regardless of the seller's actual costs.

distractors.trap: The target cost is the planned cost used as the basis for incentive sharing, not the buyer's maximum payment.
The point of total assumption is a cost level where the seller begins absorbing all overrun, not the maximum the buyer pays.
The target fee is the expected profit at target cost, not a cap on total payment.

explanation: The ceiling price is the absolute upper limit on buyer payment in an FPIF contract. Target cost and target fee are planning bases for the incentive, and the point of total assumption is a cost threshold, not a payment cap.

prompts: Early in planning a buyer wants to learn what products, services, and capabilities exist in the market before defining a requirement. Which procurement document should be issued?

correct.why: An RFI gathers market knowledge about available products and capabilities before a need is defined.

distractors.trap: An RFP asks sellers to submit a proposed solution for evaluation, which is premature before the need is defined.
An RFQ seeks price quotations for a defined requirement, but the requirement is not yet defined here.
An IFB solicits price bids for a well-defined deliverable, which does not yet exist at this stage.

explanation: When the goal is to learn what the market offers before a requirement is set, the buyer issues a request for information. Solicitation documents like RFP, RFQ, and IFB come later once the need is defined.

prompts: A buyer needs sellers to propose a solution approach and demonstrate technical expertise, not merely quote a price. Which solicitation document fits best?

correct.why: An RFP is used when the seller's approach, technical solution, and expertise matter and need to be evaluated, not just price.

distractors.trap: An RFQ mainly seeks price for a clearly specified need and does not draw out solution approach or expertise.
An IFB is a price-focused solicitation for a well-defined deliverable, selecting largely on lowest price.
An RFI only gathers market information and does not ask sellers to commit a proposed solution.

explanation: A request for proposal is appropriate when the buyer wants competing solution approaches and technical capability evaluated, whereas RFQ and IFB are price-driven instruments for well-defined needs.

prompts: Which of the following is NOT a procurement solicitation document used to obtain seller responses?

correct.why: A request for change is a change control artifact used to propose modifications to a baseline; it is not a procurement solicitation document.

distractors.trap: An RFI is a genuine procurement document used to collect market information from potential sellers.
An RFP is a procurement solicitation document asking sellers to submit proposed solutions.
An IFB is a procurement solicitation document used to request price bids for a defined deliverable.

explanation: RFI, RFP, and IFB are all solicitation documents that obtain seller responses. A request for change belongs to integrated change control, making it the trap answer that does not fit the procurement set.

prompts: When requirements are well defined and price is the primary basis of selection, which procurement document does a buyer typically use?

correct.why: An IFB is used when the deliverable is well defined and the seller is chosen mainly on price.

distractors.trap: An RFP is for cases where solution approach and seller expertise matter more than price alone.
An RFI only gathers market information and does not lead directly to selection on price.
A statement of work describes the work to be done; it is a component of a solicitation package, not the solicitation document type itself.

explanation: An invitation for bid suits a well-defined requirement where selection turns chiefly on price. An RFP is reserved for situations needing evaluation of approach and capability.

prompts: Which statement best captures the relationship between a make-or-buy decision and procurement?

correct.text: A make-or-buy decision determines whether to source externally, and procurement is the act of acquiring externally once buy is chosen
correct.why: Make-or-buy is the decision about sourcing; procurement executes the external acquisition that the buy decision triggers.

distractors.text: Procurement is the decision and make-or-buy is the act of purchasing
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; make-or-buy is the decision and procurement is the acquisition activity.

distractors.text: They are the same activity under two names
distractors.trap: They are distinct: one is a sourcing decision and the other is the acquisition process.

distractors.text: A make-or-buy decision only applies after a contract is signed
distractors.trap: Make-or-buy occurs during planning, before any contract, to decide whether to acquire externally at all.

explanation: A make-or-buy decision chooses between internal performance and external sourcing. Procurement is the broader process of actually acquiring products or services from outside once the buy option is selected.

prompts: A buyer selects a seller primarily on the quality of the proposed technical solution and negotiates price only with the top-ranked firm afterward. Which source selection method is this?

correct.why: Quality-based selection ranks sellers on technical quality first and negotiates commercial terms with the highest-ranked seller.

distractors.trap: This method scores quality and price together in the evaluation rather than deferring price to a later negotiation.
Qualifications-only chooses on competence and experience for smaller efforts, not on a scored technical proposal.
Least-cost selection picks the lowest compliant price, the opposite of leading with technical quality.

explanation: Quality-based selection evaluates technical quality first and only then negotiates price with the leading seller. Quality-and-cost-based selection instead weighs quality and price together in one score.

prompts: What distinguishes qualification criteria from source selection criteria in procurement?

correct.text: Qualification criteria decide whether a seller is eligible to be considered, while source selection criteria compare qualified sellers to choose the winner
correct.why: Qualification is a pass or fail gate for eligibility; source selection criteria then differentiate among those who pass.

distractors.text: Qualification criteria choose the winner and source selection criteria screen for eligibility
distractors.trap: This reverses the roles; eligibility screening is qualification and final comparison is source selection.

distractors.text: They are interchangeable terms for the same evaluation
distractors.trap: They serve different stages: one gates eligibility, the other compares qualified sellers.

distractors.text: Qualification criteria apply only to fixed-price contracts
distractors.trap: Qualification criteria apply to evaluating sellers across contract types, not just fixed-price.

explanation: Qualification criteria act as an eligibility gate to determine which sellers may be considered. Source selection criteria are then used to compare those qualified sellers and select the awardee.

prompts: Which document is the buyer's narrative description of the specific products, services, or results to be supplied under a procurement contract?

correct.why: The procurement statement of work narratively describes the deliverable or work to be acquired under the contract.

distractors.trap: A scope statement defines the approved project and product scope internally; it is not the procurement-specific description of contracted work.
The procurement management plan describes how procurements will be managed, not the specific item being acquired.
Bid documents are the full solicitation package sent to sellers; the procurement statement of work is just one component within it.

explanation: The procurement statement of work describes the deliverable or work to be acquired under the contract. It is distinct from the scope statement, the procurement management plan, and the broader bid documents package.

prompts: A customer discovers a defect shortly after delivery and the seller repairs it under a contractual promise covering the deliverable for a defined period after acceptance. Which contractual provision applies?

correct.why: A warranty is the seller's contractual assurance that the deliverable will perform as promised for a period after acceptance, obligating repair of covered defects.

distractors.trap: An SLA defines ongoing service performance targets, not a post-acceptance promise to repair a defective deliverable.
Retainage is a withheld portion of payment released on completion, not a defect-repair obligation.
A claim is a disputed assertion of entitlement; here the seller is honoring an agreed obligation, not contesting one.

explanation: A warranty obligates the seller to correct covered defects in a deliverable for a stated period after acceptance. SLAs, retainage, and claims address different contractual concerns.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Warranty repairs and rework triggered by defects found after delivery fall under which category of the cost of quality?

correct.text: Cost of nonconformance, specifically external failure costs
correct.why: Costs incurred because of failures found after delivery are nonconformance costs, and those reaching the customer are external failure costs.

distractors.trap: Conformance costs are spent up front to prevent and detect defects, not to fix failures after they occur.
Prevention costs are conformance spending to stop defects before they happen, not post-delivery repair.
Appraisal costs are conformance spending on inspection and testing, not the cost of failures found by the customer.

explanation: Money spent because defects occurred is the cost of nonconformance; when the customer finds the defect after delivery it is an external failure cost. Prevention and appraisal are conformance costs spent to avoid failures.

prompts: In which form of alternative dispute resolution does a neutral third party actually decide the outcome, which the parties agree in advance to accept?

correct.why: In arbitration a neutral arbitrator hears the dispute and renders a decision that the parties have agreed to accept.

distractors.trap: A mediator only facilitates the parties toward their own agreement and does not decide the outcome.
Negotiation is direct bargaining between the parties with no neutral decision-maker imposing a result.
A dispute review board typically helps prevent and resolve disputes, often advisory, rather than imposing a binding decision in the arbitral sense.

explanation: Arbitration submits a dispute to a neutral third party whose decision the parties accept. Mediation differs because the mediator facilitates rather than decides.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes a claim from a dispute in contract administration?

correct.text: A claim is an assertion or request for compensation or entitlement, and a dispute is the unresolved disagreement that remains when the parties cannot settle the claim
correct.why: A claim asserts entitlement; if the parties cannot agree on it, the unsettled disagreement becomes a dispute.

distractors.text: A dispute is the initial request and a claim is the unresolved disagreement
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; the request is the claim and the unresolved disagreement is the dispute.

distractors.text: A claim and a dispute are identical and used interchangeably
distractors.trap: They mark different stages: an asserted request versus an unresolved disagreement over it.

distractors.text: A claim is always resolved by a change request rather than negotiation
distractors.trap: A claim is a contested entitlement, not a routine baseline change; it is not resolved simply by issuing a change request.

explanation: A claim is a request or assertion of entitlement, such as for added compensation. A dispute is what exists when the parties cannot agree and the claim remains unresolved.

prompts: At the start of a large construction project the parties appoint a standing panel of neutral experts to help prevent disagreements and resolve them as they arise during execution. What is this mechanism called?

correct.why: A dispute review board is a neutral panel set up at project start to help avoid disputes and resolve them throughout execution.

distractors.trap: Arbitration is invoked after a dispute has formed and renders a decision; it is not a standing panel established at the outset.
Mediation is usually introduced after a dispute already exists, rather than a panel appointed at the start to prevent disputes.
A bidder conference clarifies requirements with prospective sellers before bids; it has nothing to do with resolving disputes.

explanation: A dispute review board is established early and remains engaged through the project to head off and resolve disputes. Arbitration and mediation are typically engaged only once a dispute has arisen.

prompts: A negotiator identifies the best course of action available if the current negotiation fails to reach a deal. What is this fallback called?

correct.why: BATNA is the best alternative to a negotiated agreement, the fallback a party pursues if no deal is reached.

distractors.trap: The zone of possible agreement is the overlap range where a deal can be struck, not the fallback if talks fail.
A reservation price is the walk-away threshold value, not the alternative course of action pursued instead of a deal.
Fait accompli is a tactic of presenting an issue as already decided, not a fallback option.

explanation: BATNA is the best alternative a party can pursue if the negotiation collapses. It anchors how far a negotiator is willing to go, and it differs from the ZOPA, which is the range where agreement is possible.

prompts: During a negotiation the most the buyer will pay and the least the seller will accept overlap, creating a band in which both could agree. What is this overlap called?

correct.why: The zone of possible agreement is the bargaining range where the parties' limits overlap and a deal is feasible.

distractors.trap: A BATNA is the fallback if no deal is reached, not the overlap range where a deal is possible.
A ceiling price is a contractual payment cap in a specific contract, not a negotiation overlap range.
A share ratio splits cost variances in an incentive contract; it is unrelated to a negotiation bargaining range.

explanation: The zone of possible agreement is the overlap between the buyer's maximum and the seller's minimum, the band within which a mutually acceptable deal can be made.

prompts: During contract talks one party declares a key term already settled and not open for discussion, in order to narrow what gets negotiated. Which negotiation tactic is this?

correct.why: Fait accompli presents something as already decided and beyond discussion to shrink the scope of negotiation.

distractors.trap: A BATNA is a fallback option, not a tactic of declaring a term closed to discussion.
Good-faith negotiation keeps issues genuinely open, the opposite of declaring one closed.
A bidder conference is a pre-bid clarification meeting, not an in-negotiation pressure tactic.

explanation: Fait accompli is a tactic of treating an issue as a done deal to take it off the table. It contrasts with good-faith negotiation, in which issues remain genuinely open.

prompts: In a procurement relationship, which pairing correctly matches each role to its function?

correct.text: The buyer acquires and pays for the work, while the seller provides the contracted product or service
correct.why: The buyer is the acquirer who pays; the seller is the provider who delivers the contracted goods or services.

distractors.text: The buyer provides the product while the seller pays for it
distractors.trap: This reverses the roles; the buyer pays and the seller provides.

distractors.text: The buyer and seller both provide goods to a third party
distractors.trap: Procurement is a two-party exchange in which one acquires and one provides, not joint provision to a third party.

distractors.text: The seller acquires resources and the buyer performs the work
distractors.trap: This inverts both roles; the buyer acquires and the seller performs the contracted work.

explanation: By definition the buyer acquires and pays for products, services, or results, and the seller provides them under the procurement arrangement.

prompts: How does a contract differ from an agreement?

correct.text: A contract is a legally binding type of agreement, while agreement is the broader term that can include nonbinding arrangements
correct.why: A contract carries enforceable legal obligations, whereas agreement is the umbrella term that also covers nonbinding understandings.

distractors.text: An agreement is always legally binding while a contract may be informal
distractors.trap: This reverses the relationship; the contract is the binding form and agreement is the broader, possibly nonbinding term.
They differ in scope: every contract is an agreement, but not every agreement is a binding contract.

distractors.text: A contract cannot contain terms and conditions but an agreement can
distractors.trap: Contracts in fact specify enforceable terms and conditions; this statement is incorrect.

explanation: A contract is a mutually binding agreement that creates enforceable obligations. Agreement is the broader category, which can include both binding and nonbinding arrangements.

prompts: A project buys insurance so that the financial impact of a specific threat would be borne by an insurer rather than the project. Which risk response strategy does this illustrate?

correct.why: Buying insurance shifts the financial impact of a threat to a third party, which is the transfer strategy.

distractors.trap: Mitigation keeps the risk with the project while reducing its probability or impact, rather than shifting the impact to a third party.

distractors.text: Risk acceptance with a contingency reserve
distractors.trap: Acceptance retains the risk and funds it internally, the opposite of moving the impact to an insurer.
Avoidance eliminates the threat by changing the plan, not by paying a third party to bear the impact.

explanation: Insurance is a classic risk transfer mechanism: a premium is paid so a third party absorbs the defined financial consequences of a threat. Mitigation, acceptance, and avoidance keep or eliminate the risk differently.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes risk transference from risk sharing?

correct.text: Transference shifts the impact of a threat to a third party, while sharing allocates a portion of ownership or outcome with another party and often applies to opportunities
correct.why: Transfer moves a threat's impact wholly to another party; sharing distributes part of an upside or downside with a partner.

distractors.text: Transference applies only to opportunities and sharing only to threats
distractors.trap: Transference is a threat strategy, and sharing is most associated with opportunities; this reverses them.

distractors.text: They are the same strategy with different labels
distractors.trap: They differ: one fully shifts a threat, the other distributes part of an outcome with a partner.

distractors.text: Sharing always eliminates the risk while transference increases it
distractors.trap: Neither eliminates nor inflates the risk; both reassign responsibility or participation for it.

explanation: Risk transference shifts a threat's impact to a third party, commonly via contract or insurance. Risk sharing allocates a portion of ownership or outcome with another party and is frequently used for opportunities.

prompts: Which statement correctly contrasts buying insurance with holding a contingency reserve as ways to handle a risk?

correct.text: Insurance transfers defined financial exposure to a third party, while a contingency reserve retains the risk and sets aside funds internally
correct.why: Insurance moves the exposure off the project to an insurer; a contingency reserve keeps the risk and funds it from within.

distractors.text: Both insurance and a contingency reserve transfer the risk to a third party
distractors.trap: A contingency reserve does not transfer the risk; it retains it and funds it internally.

distractors.text: A contingency reserve transfers risk while insurance retains it
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; insurance transfers and the reserve retains.

distractors.text: Insurance and a contingency reserve both eliminate the underlying risk
distractors.trap: Neither eliminates the risk; one transfers the financial impact and the other funds the retained risk.

explanation: Insurance is a transfer mechanism that moves financial exposure to an insurer. A contingency reserve is an acceptance mechanism that retains the risk and earmarks internal funds to cover it.

prompts: A procurement team classifies its purchases by profit impact and supply risk in order to choose the right sourcing strategy for each category. Which model are they using?

correct.why: The Kraljic matrix classifies purchases by profit impact and supply risk to guide category sourcing strategy.

distractors.trap: Make-or-buy analysis decides whether to produce internally or acquire externally, not how to classify external purchases by impact and risk.
A decision tree maps branching choices and probabilistic outcomes, not a purchasing portfolio classification.
SWOT assesses strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats, not purchases by profit impact and supply risk.

explanation: The Kraljic matrix is a procurement portfolio model that maps purchases on profit impact and supply risk so the team can tailor a sourcing strategy to each quadrant.

prompts: A custom turbine takes nine months to manufacture and deliver, so it must be ordered very early no matter when its installation is scheduled. What is such an item called?

correct.why: A long-lead item needs significant advance time to procure or produce and so must be identified and ordered early in planning.

distractors.trap: A critical path activity is defined by its effect on total project duration, not by a long acquisition time on its own.
A just-in-time item is delivered only when needed to minimize inventory, the opposite of ordering far in advance.
A sunk cost is money already spent and unrecoverable, not an item with a long procurement lead time.

explanation: A long-lead item requires extended time to procure, produce, or deliver, so it is identified early and ordered well ahead of need to protect the schedule.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes Control Procurements from Close Procurements?

correct.text: Control Procurements manages performance while the contract is active, and Close Procurements formally completes and closes the contract after the work is finished
correct.why: Control happens during contract performance to monitor and correct; closure finalizes the contract once the work is complete.

distractors.text: Control Procurements closes contracts and Close Procurements monitors active ones
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; monitoring active contracts is control and finalizing them is closure.

distractors.text: Both processes occur only after all contract work is complete
distractors.trap: Control Procurements runs throughout active performance, not only after the work is done.

distractors.text: Close Procurements manages claims while the contract is active
distractors.trap: Claims may be settled during closure, but managing the live contract relationship is the role of Control Procurements.

explanation: Control Procurements manages the contract relationship and performance while work is ongoing, making corrections as needed. Close Procurements formally completes and closes each contract after the work is finished.

prompts: Before sellers prepare their bids, the buyer holds a single meeting with all prospective sellers to ensure a common understanding of the procurement requirement. What is this meeting called?

correct.why: A bidder conference is held with prospective sellers before bids are prepared so all share a common understanding of the requirement.

distractors.trap: Proposal evaluation happens after bids are submitted to compare and score them, not before to clarify the requirement.
Source selection is the later evaluation and choice of a seller, not the pre-bid clarification meeting.
Negotiation occurs later to reach agreement with a selected seller, not as an open pre-bid clarification with all sellers.

explanation: A bidder conference, sometimes called a contractor or vendor conference, ensures every prospective seller has the same clear understanding of the requirement before preparing a bid or proposal.

prompts: Select all of the following contract types that belong to the cost-reimbursable family.

correct.why: CPFF reimburses allowable costs plus a fixed fee, a cost-reimbursable type.
CPIF reimburses allowable costs and varies the fee by performance, a cost-reimbursable type.
CPAF reimburses allowable costs plus a buyer-evaluated award fee, a cost-reimbursable type.

distractors.trap: FFP sets one price regardless of cost; it is a fixed-price contract, not cost-reimbursable.
FPIF is a fixed-price contract with an incentive formula, not a cost-reimbursable type.
T and M is a hybrid billing actual hours and materials at set rates, not a cost-reimbursable contract.

explanation: The cost-reimbursable family pays the seller's allowable actual costs plus a fee and includes CPFF, CPIF, and CPAF. FFP and FPIF are fixed-price, and T and M is a hybrid.

prompts: Select all of the following that are recognized forms of alternative dispute resolution used to settle contract disputes outside the courts.

correct.why: Mediation is an ADR method in which a neutral facilitates the parties toward their own settlement.
Arbitration is an ADR method in which a neutral renders a decision the parties accept.
A dispute review board is an ADR mechanism of neutral experts that helps prevent and resolve disputes during the project.

distractors.trap: A bidder conference is a pre-bid clarification meeting, not a dispute resolution method.
Source selection is the evaluation and choice of a seller, unrelated to resolving disputes.
Kitting groups materials into preassembled sets for execution efficiency, not a dispute resolution method.

explanation: Alternative dispute resolution avoids litigation through methods such as mediation, arbitration, and standing dispute review boards. Bidder conferences, source selection, and kitting are unrelated procurement activities.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A team is reviewing whether the process used to build deliverables is sound and likely to produce conforming results, rather than checking the deliverables themselves. Which activity best describes this?
