correct.why: Quality assurance is process-focused: it provides confidence that the work and processes will satisfy quality requirements.

distractors.trap: Quality control inspects the deliverables and results themselves, not the adequacy of the process producing them.
Inspection examines a specific work product for conformance; it is a control technique, not the broad process-confidence activity.
Validation confirms the deliverable meets intended needs; it is outcome-focused, not process-assurance.

explanation: Distinguish QA (process: building confidence the process is right) from QC (product: inspecting the outputs). The stem describes process adequacy, so it is QA.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes verification from validation?

correct.text: Verification checks conformance to specifications; validation checks fitness for intended use
correct.why: Verification asks are we building it right (against specs); validation asks are we building the right thing (meets the need).

distractors.text: Verification confirms the customer accepts the deliverable; validation checks the spec internally
distractors.trap: Reverses the pair and confuses validation with formal acceptance.

distractors.text: Verification is performed by the customer; validation is performed internally by the team
distractors.trap: Validation is typically the customer-facing fit-for-use check; this inverts who does what.

distractors.text: Verification and validation are interchangeable terms for inspection
distractors.trap: They are distinct evaluation activities, not synonyms for inspection.

explanation: Verification = conformance to specifications (building it right). Validation = fitness for intended use / meeting the need (building the right thing).

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A scale consistently reports 70 kg for a 50 kg weight every time it is used. How would you describe this instrument?

correct.why: Repeated readings cluster tightly (precise) but sit far from the true value of 50 kg (not accurate).

distractors.trap: Accuracy is closeness to the true value, which fails here; the consistency is precision, not accuracy.
It cannot be accurate when every reading is 20 kg off the true value.
It is precise because the readings repeat consistently; only accuracy is missing.

explanation: Precision is repeatability of results; accuracy is closeness to the true value. Consistent-but-wrong equals precise but not accurate.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A software product is deliberately released as a low-feature, low-cost edition that still works flawlessly for its requirements. In PMI terms, this product has which characteristics?

correct.why: Grade is the category or feature level chosen; quality is how well requirements are met. Few features at flawless conformance is low grade, high quality.

distractors.trap: Reverses the pair: flawless conformance is high quality, and fewer features is low grade, not the other way around.
Working flawlessly to requirements is high quality, so quality is not low.
A deliberately low-feature edition is low grade, not high grade.

explanation: Grade ranks technical characteristics/features; quality measures conformance to requirements. Low grade with high quality is acceptable; low quality is always a problem.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Money spent on inspecting and testing finished units to find defects before shipment is classified as which cost of quality?

correct.why: Appraisal cost is the cost of evaluating products to determine whether they conform to requirements, such as inspection and testing.

distractors.trap: Prevention cost is spent to avoid defects before they occur (training, process design), not to evaluate finished units.
Internal failure is rework or scrap after a defect is found, not the act of inspecting to find it.
External failure occurs after the customer receives the product (warranty, recalls), not during pre-shipment inspection.

explanation: Inspection and testing to detect defects is appraisal cost (a cost of conformance). Prevention avoids defects; failure costs occur after defects exist.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A customer reports a defect after the product has shipped and the company pays warranty and recall costs. Which cost of quality category is this?

correct.why: External failure cost is incurred when nonconformance reaches the customer, such as warranty claims, recalls, and lost goodwill.

distractors.trap: Internal failure is found before the customer receives the product (scrap, rework); here the customer already has it.
Appraisal is the cost of evaluating conformance, not the cost of a failure that escaped to the customer.
Prevention is spent up front to stop defects; a post-delivery warranty claim is the opposite end of the spectrum.

explanation: Failure costs split into internal (found before delivery) and external (found by the customer). Warranty and recall costs are external failure, a cost of nonconformance.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Select the two categories that make up the cost of conformance (the money spent to prevent and detect poor quality).

correct.why: Prevention cost (training, documenting processes) is spent to keep defects from occurring and is a cost of conformance.
Appraisal cost (inspection, testing) is spent to evaluate conformance and is the other cost of conformance.

distractors.trap: Internal failure (rework, scrap before delivery) is a cost of NONconformance, not conformance.
External failure (warranty, recalls, lost business) is a cost of NONconformance.

explanation: Cost of conformance = prevention + appraisal. Cost of nonconformance = internal failure + external failure.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A control chart shows points randomly scattered within the control limits, reflecting the inherent, ever-present noise of a stable process. This variation comes from a:

correct.why: Common cause variation is inherent to a stable process and arises from the system as designed, not a specific event.

distractors.trap: Special cause variation comes from a specific, identifiable, unusual event and signals the process is out of control.
Assignable cause is another name for special cause; it points to a specific identifiable source, not inherent noise.
Points within the limits are not breaching anything; this describes normal in-control variation.

explanation: Common cause = inherent system noise in a stable process. Special (assignable) cause = a specific identifiable disturbance requiring investigation.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: An independent reviewer examines whether the team is actually following its defined processes and procedures, rather than examining any single deliverable. This is best described as a/an:

correct.why: An audit is a structured, independent examination of whether activities comply with processes, policies, and procedures.

distractors.trap: Inspection examines the product or deliverable itself for conformance, not whether the process is being followed.
Validation checks that a deliverable meets the intended need; it is not a process-compliance review.
A walkthrough is a peer review of a work product, narrower than an independent process-compliance audit.

explanation: Audit = process/compliance focus (independent examination of how work is done). Inspection = product focus (examining the deliverable itself).

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Which approach reflects the principle prevention over inspection?

correct.text: Designing the process so defects are not introduced in the first place
correct.why: Prevention keeps errors out of the work up front, which is cheaper and preferred over catching them later.

distractors.text: Adding more inspectors at the end of the line to catch defects
distractors.trap: This is inspection (detection after the fact), the very thing prevention is preferred over.

distractors.text: Increasing the sample size used during final testing
distractors.trap: Larger samples improve detection, but detection is still inspection, not prevention.

distractors.text: Reworking defective units faster once they are found
distractors.trap: Faster rework addresses defects that already exist; prevention stops them from occurring.

explanation: Prevention (keeping defects out) is preferred over inspection (finding defects after they occur) because preventing a defect is cheaper than detecting and fixing it.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A team inspects each unit and records only a pass or fail result with no measured value. Which sampling type is being used?

correct.why: Attribute sampling records a conforming or nonconforming (pass/fail) result for each item rather than a measured magnitude.

distractors.trap: Variable sampling records a measured value on a continuous scale (length, weight), not a simple pass/fail.
Stratification is about dividing a population into subgroups before sampling, not about pass/fail versus measured data.
This is a probability property, not a category of quality sampling.

explanation: Attribute sampling = conforms or not (pass/fail, discrete). Variable sampling = degree of conformance on a continuous measured scale.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: In Lean thinking, which of the following is the odd one out because it is NOT a category of waste to be eliminated?

correct.text: Value-added processing that transforms the product per customer requirements
correct.why: Value-added work is exactly what Lean preserves; it is not waste. The other options are recognized Lean wastes.

distractors.text: Waiting and idle time between steps
distractors.trap: Waiting is a classic Lean waste, so it belongs in the waste group, not the odd one out.

distractors.text: Excess inventory held just in case
distractors.trap: Inventory is one of the canonical Lean wastes.
Defects are a Lean waste; correcting them consumes resources without adding value.

explanation: Lean maximizes value while eliminating waste (waiting, inventory, defects, overproduction, motion, transport, over-processing). Value-added transformation is the opposite of waste.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A Six Sigma team is improving an EXISTING process that produces too many defects, working through Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control. Which methodology are they following?

correct.why: DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control) is used to improve an existing process that is underperforming.

distractors.trap: DMADV (Define-Measure-Analyze-Design-Verify) is for designing a new process or product, not improving an existing one.
PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is Deming's general improvement cycle, not the five-phase Six Sigma sequence described.
This is not a standard Six Sigma methodology; it is a distractor built from familiar letters.

explanation: DMAIC improves an existing process; DMADV (Design for Six Sigma) creates a new product or process. The stem describes fixing an existing process, so DMAIC.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A team holds frequent retrospectives and makes many small incremental changes to steadily improve their workflow. Which Lean/quality concept does this best illustrate?

correct.why: Kaizen is continuous improvement through small, ongoing, incremental changes to processes and outcomes.

distractors.trap: Kanban is a visual flow and work-in-progress management method, not the philosophy of small incremental improvements.
Kaikaku refers to radical, breakthrough change, the opposite of Kaizen incrementalism.
Gemba means going to the place where work happens to observe; it is not the incremental-improvement practice itself.

explanation: Kaizen is the practice of continual, small, incremental improvement. It is a specific pattern within the broader idea of continuous improvement.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Fishbone diagram, Ishikawa diagram, and cause-and-effect diagram are best described as:

correct.text: Three names for the same root-cause analysis tool
correct.why: Fishbone, Ishikawa, and cause-and-effect diagram are synonyms for one tool that organizes possible causes around an effect.

distractors.text: Three distinct tools used at different stages of quality control
distractors.trap: They are not distinct tools; they are interchangeable names for the same diagram.

distractors.text: A diagram, a chart, and a checklist respectively
distractors.trap: All three are the same diagram; none is a chart or checklist.

distractors.text: Two quality tools and one statistical sampling method
distractors.trap: None of the three is a sampling method; all three are the same cause-and-effect tool.

explanation: Fishbone = Ishikawa = cause-and-effect diagram: one tool, three names, used to trace an effect back to candidate root causes.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Which quality thinker is most associated with the assertion that quality is free?

correct.why: Crosby argued quality is free because preventing defects costs less than the price of nonconformance, and promoted zero defects.

distractors.trap: Juran is associated with fitness for use and the quality trilogy, not the phrase quality is free.
Deming is tied to PDCA, the 14 points, and reducing variation, not the quality is free slogan.
Ishikawa is known for the fishbone diagram and quality circles, not quality is free.

explanation: Crosby: quality is free and zero defects. Juran: fitness for use. Deming: PDCA and variation reduction. Ishikawa: fishbone diagram.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Which quality pioneer defined quality as fitness for use and is known for the quality trilogy of planning, control, and improvement?

correct.why: Juran defined quality as fitness for use and developed the quality trilogy and the Pareto principle in quality.

distractors.trap: Crosby is known for quality is free and zero defects and defined quality as conformance to requirements, not fitness for use.
Deming championed PDCA and variation reduction; fitness for use is Juran.
Shewhart pioneered control charts and the PDCA precursor cycle, not the fitness-for-use definition.

explanation: Juran = fitness for use and the quality trilogy. Crosby = conformance to requirements and quality is free. The fitness-for-use phrasing is the Juran tell.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: The Plan-Do-Check-Act improvement cycle is most directly associated with which figure?

correct.why: Deming popularized the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle (building on Shewhart) as the engine of continuous improvement.

distractors.trap: Crosby is tied to zero defects and quality is free, not the PDCA cycle.
Ishikawa is known for the fishbone diagram and quality circles, not PDCA.
Juran is associated with fitness for use and the quality trilogy, not PDCA.

explanation: PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the Deming (Shewhart-Deming) cycle for continuous improvement.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A process is producing parts whose natural variation, as measured by the process itself, falls inside the band the design engineer requires for the part to be acceptable. The engineer-required band is known as the:

correct.why: Specification limits are the externally required tolerance the product must meet to be acceptable, set by the customer or design.

distractors.trap: Control limits reflect the natural variation of the process itself (calculated from data), not the externally required tolerance.
A confidence interval is a statistical estimate range, not the required acceptance band for a product.
Tolerance stack-up is the accumulation of tolerances across assembled parts, not the single required acceptance band.

explanation: Specification limits = required acceptance band (voice of the customer). Control limits = natural process variation (voice of the process), derived from the data.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: You need to show how a process behaves over time and whether it is stable, plotting data points against a centerline and upper and lower bounds. Which tool fits?

correct.why: A control chart plots process data over time against control limits and a centerline to detect trends and instability.

distractors.trap: A histogram shows the frequency distribution of values but loses the time order, so it cannot show stability over time.
A Pareto chart ranks categories by frequency to prioritize causes; it does not track behavior over time.
A scatter diagram shows the relationship between two variables, not a single process tracked over time.

explanation: Control chart = process behavior over time vs limits. Histogram = distribution snapshot. The time dimension and limits point to the control chart.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A team finds that 80 percent of customer complaints trace to about 20 percent of the defect types, and wants a chart that ranks the causes so they can attack the vital few first. Which tool should they use?

correct.why: A Pareto chart orders categories from most to least frequent so effort focuses on the vital few causes producing most of the effect.

distractors.trap: A histogram shows a frequency distribution but does not rank categories to highlight the most significant contributors.
A control chart tracks process stability over time; it does not prioritize causes by frequency.
A scatter diagram explores correlation between two variables, not the ranking of defect causes.

explanation: The Pareto chart applies the 80/20 principle, ordering causes by frequency so teams target the vital few. A histogram shows distribution without that prioritizing order.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Which statement best distinguishes Manage Quality from Control Quality?

correct.text: Manage Quality improves and assures the processes used to create deliverables; Control Quality inspects the actual deliverables and results
correct.why: Manage Quality is the process/assurance side; Control Quality is the inspection/verification of outputs.

distractors.text: Manage Quality inspects deliverables; Control Quality writes the quality policy
distractors.trap: Reverses the pair and miscasts Control Quality; inspecting deliverables is Control Quality.

distractors.text: Manage Quality occurs only in closing; Control Quality occurs only in planning
distractors.trap: Both are executing/monitoring activities; the timing claim is wrong.

distractors.text: They are the same process under two names
distractors.trap: They are distinct processes: one is process-focused assurance, the other is product-focused control.

explanation: Manage Quality (assurance) improves processes; Control Quality measures and inspects outputs against requirements. Process versus product.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A specific scratch on one delivered laptop case is an example of a defect. The broader fact that the case fails to meet the finish requirement is best termed:

correct.why: Nonconformance is the broader condition of failing to meet a requirement; the scratch is one specific defect within it.

distractors.trap: Rework is the action taken to fix the item, not the condition of failing the requirement.
Corrective action addresses the cause of a variance; it is a response, not the failure-to-meet-requirement condition.
Variance is a general gap between planned and actual; nonconformance is the specific quality term for not meeting a requirement.

explanation: A defect is a specific flaw; nonconformance is the broader state of not meeting a requirement. The defect is an instance of nonconformance.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A schedule variance has already appeared, and the manager takes deliberate action to realign work with the project management plan. This is best described as:

correct.why: Corrective action realigns performance of the work with the plan after a variance has occurred.

distractors.trap: Preventive action reduces the likelihood of a FUTURE variance; here the variance already exists.
Defect repair corrects a flaw in a deliverable, not a schedule variance against the plan.
Approval is a control decision; the action itself that realigns past variance is corrective action.

explanation: Corrective action fixes an existing deviation; preventive action heads off a future one. The variance has already occurred, so it is corrective.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A coded module has a bug. The action of fixing that specific bug in the module is which of the following?

correct.why: Defect repair is the action taken to correct a specific defect in a component or deliverable.

distractors.trap: Corrective action more broadly realigns work with the plan or addresses the cause of a variance, not fixing one specific defect.
Preventive action reduces the chance of a future problem; the bug already exists and is being fixed.
Refactoring improves internal structure without changing external behavior; fixing a bug changes the wrong behavior.

explanation: Defect repair = fix a specific flaw. Corrective action = broader realignment to the plan. Fixing one bug is defect repair.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: An analyst wants to see whether longer training hours are related to fewer defects by plotting each pair of values as points. Which tool displays this relationship between two variables?

correct.why: A scatter diagram plots paired values of two variables to reveal their relationship or correlation.

distractors.trap: A control chart tracks one variable over time against limits, not the relationship between two variables.
A histogram shows the distribution of a single variable, not a relationship between two.
A check sheet tallies occurrences of categories; it does not plot the correlation of two variables.

explanation: A scatter diagram reveals correlation between two variables; a histogram shows distribution of one. Two variables and paired points point to scatter.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A tester uses a structured form to tally how often each defect type appears as units come off the line, so patterns can later be analyzed. Which tool is this?

correct.why: A check sheet (tally sheet) collects and counts observed data consistently so frequency and patterns can be analyzed.

distractors.trap: A checklist verifies whether required steps or items were completed; it does not tally observed frequency of defect types.
A Pareto chart ranks already-collected data; the check sheet is the tool used to collect the tally in the first place.
A quality report communicates and interprets quality issues; it is not the data-collection tally form.

explanation: A check sheet collects and tallies observed data; a checklist confirms completion of required items. The stem describes tallying frequency, so check sheet.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Which of the following is the odd one out because it is NOT one of the seven basic quality tools?

correct.why: The affinity diagram is a grouping/ideation tool, not part of the seven basic quality tools.

distractors.trap: The control chart is one of the seven basic quality tools.
The Pareto chart is one of the seven basic quality tools.
The cause-and-effect (fishbone) diagram is one of the seven basic quality tools.

explanation: The seven basic quality tools include cause-and-effect, control charts, check sheets, histograms, Pareto charts, scatter diagrams, and flowcharts. Affinity diagrams are not in that set.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Quality requirements state what must be achieved. What do quality metrics define in relation to them?

correct.text: How the attribute will be measured and the allowable values
correct.why: Quality metrics describe the specific attribute and how it will be measured to verify compliance with quality requirements.

distractors.text: The conditions that must be met before a deliverable is accepted
distractors.trap: Those are acceptance criteria, not quality metrics; metrics define measurement, not the acceptance gate.

distractors.text: The organization-wide direction on quality set by top management
distractors.trap: That is the quality policy; metrics are project- and product-level measurement definitions.

distractors.text: The recorded results produced by inspecting the deliverable
distractors.trap: Those are quality control measurements (the actual data); metrics define what and how to measure beforehand.

explanation: Quality requirements = what; quality metrics = how it will be measured. Quality control measurements are the resulting recorded data.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: The overall intentions and direction of an organization regarding quality, formally expressed by top management, is captured in the:

correct.why: The quality policy states the organization-wide intentions and direction on quality, set by top management.

distractors.trap: The quality management plan explains how a specific project will implement quality; it does not set organization-wide direction.
Quality metrics define how attributes are measured, not the organization-wide quality direction.
Quality objectives are specific measurable targets, not the top-management statement of overall direction.

explanation: Quality policy = organization-wide direction from top management. The quality management plan is the project-level implementation of that policy.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A nonconforming component is brought back into full compliance with its original requirement. Which term most precisely describes this action?

correct.why: Rework brings a defective or nonconforming item back into compliance with its original requirements or specifications.

distractors.trap: Repair modifies a defective item to make it usable but may not fully restore original conformity, unlike rework.
Refactoring improves internal structure without changing external behavior; it does not address a nonconforming requirement.
Scrap discards the item rather than bringing it into compliance.

explanation: Rework restores conformance to the original requirement; repair makes an item usable but may not fully restore original conformity.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A statistician sets up controlled experiments varying temperature and pressure together to learn which factors most influence a coating defect rate. Which quality technique is this?

correct.why: Design of experiments is a statistical method to identify which factors influence specific variables of a product or process.

distractors.trap: Design for X optimizes a design for a target characteristic such as manufacturability; it does not test which factors drive an outcome.
Statistical sampling inspects a subset to draw conclusions about a population; it does not deliberately vary factors to test influence.
Root cause analysis investigates why a specific problem occurred; it is not a designed factorial experiment.

explanation: Design of experiments deliberately varies factors to quantify their influence on an outcome. Design for X optimizes a design toward a target attribute.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: In Scrum, which term describes the shared standard that EVERY increment must meet to be considered complete, rather than the conditions tied to a single requirement?

correct.why: Definition of Done is the common completion standard applied to all increments or backlog items.

distractors.trap: Acceptance criteria define conditions for one specific requirement or backlog item, not the shared standard for every increment.
A quality metric defines how an attribute is measured; it is not the team-wide completion standard.
Evaluation criteria are used to assess or compare options; they are not the increment completion standard.

explanation: Definition of Done = shared completion standard for all increments. Acceptance criteria = conditions for an individual requirement or item.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A product conforms perfectly to its written requirements, yet the customer feels the project did not deliver what they truly wanted. Which concept captures this gap?

correct.why: Customer satisfaction reflects the customer overall perception of value and met expectations, which can differ from mere conformance.

distractors.trap: Quality is conformance to requirements and fitness for use; here conformance was met, so quality is not the gap.
Grade is the feature/category level chosen; it does not capture the customer perception of value.
Compliance is adherence to mandated rules and laws, not the customer perception of whether expectations were met.

explanation: Conformance can be achieved while customer satisfaction is not, because satisfaction reflects perceived value and expectations beyond the written requirements.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Process capability and process performance are easily confused. Which statement correctly separates them?

correct.text: Process capability is what the process can achieve under stable conditions; process performance is how it is actually performing now
correct.why: Capability is the potential of a stable process to meet requirements; performance is its current actual behavior.

distractors.text: Process capability is the current actual output; process performance is the theoretical maximum
distractors.trap: Reverses the pair: capability is the stable potential, performance is the current actual.

distractors.text: Process capability measures cost; process performance measures schedule
distractors.trap: Neither term is about cost or schedule; both concern quality variation and conformance ability.

distractors.text: They are interchangeable terms for the same statistical index
distractors.trap: They are distinct: one is stable-condition potential, the other is current actual performance.

explanation: Capability = what a stable process is able to achieve versus requirements. Performance = how the process is actually behaving right now.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Inspecting and testing only a representative subset of items to draw conclusions about the entire population is known as:

correct.why: Statistical sampling examines part of a population to infer conclusions about the whole, often to reduce inspection cost.

distractors.trap: Inspecting every item is the opposite of sampling a subset.
An audit examines process compliance, not a statistical subset of product units.
Design of experiments varies factors to test influence; it is not subset inspection to infer population quality.

explanation: Statistical sampling inspects a representative subset to draw population conclusions, trading some certainty for lower inspection cost.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: The full framework covering prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs across the product life is collectively called:

correct.why: Cost of quality is the total framework of conformance (prevention, appraisal) and nonconformance (internal, external failure) costs over the product life.

distractors.trap: Quality control is the activity of inspecting outputs, a part of the picture, not the full cost framework.
The cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget, not the quality cost framework.
Cost-benefit analysis compares costs to benefits of a decision; it is not the four-category quality cost model.

explanation: Cost of quality bundles all conformance and nonconformance costs over the product life into one framework: prevention, appraisal, internal failure, external failure.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Quality assurance relies on a particular technique to provide independent confidence that processes are being followed. Which technique pairs most directly with quality assurance?

correct.why: A quality audit is the independent review used within quality assurance to confirm processes comply with policies and procedures.

distractors.trap: Inspection checks the deliverable for conformance and pairs with quality control, not assurance.
Statistical sampling supports inspecting outputs (control), not assuring the process.
A control chart monitors output variation over time; it is a control tool, not the assurance audit.

explanation: Quality assurance is process-focused and uses quality audits. Quality control is product-focused and uses inspection, sampling, and control charts.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A team adds a late, dedicated timebox at the end of development to stabilize, integrate, and fix accumulated defects before release. This timebox is known as a:

correct.why: A hardening sprint is a timebox used to stabilize, integrate, and fix defects to prepare an increment for release.

distractors.trap: Definition of Done aims to build release-quality into every increment, avoiding the need to concentrate fixes later.
A retrospective is a process-improvement meeting, not a development timebox for stabilizing the product.
Refactoring restructures code without changing behavior; it is not a dedicated stabilization timebox before release.

explanation: A hardening sprint concentrates stabilization and defect-fixing in a later timebox. A strong Definition of Done reduces the need for one.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Two testing levels are easily confused. Which one verifies that combined components work together correctly through their interfaces?

correct.why: Integration testing focuses on the interactions and interfaces between combined components or subsystems.

distractors.trap: System testing evaluates the complete integrated system against overall requirements, not specifically the interfaces between components.
A unit test exercises a single component in isolation, not the interactions between combined components.
Acceptance testing confirms the solution meets the customer needs for sign-off, not component interface interactions.

explanation: Integration test = interactions across combined components. System test = the whole integrated system against broad requirements.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A developer chooses a quick, expedient design now, knowing it will cost more rework later even though no visible defect exists yet. What is being incurred?

correct.why: Technical debt is the implied future cost of rework created by choosing an expedient solution over a more robust one.

distractors.trap: A defect is a present flaw that fails a requirement; technical debt may exist with no visible defect yet.
Nonconformance means a requirement is currently unmet; here the work may still meet requirements while carrying future cost.
Failure cost is incurred once nonconformance occurs; technical debt is the latent future burden before any failure.

explanation: Technical debt is the future rework cost of expedient choices, distinct from a present defect or nonconformance that already fails a requirement.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: Which pairing of process stability versus process capability is correct?

correct.text: Stability is about predictable variation over time; capability is about meeting specification limits
correct.why: Stability means only expected variation over time; capability means the process can meet the required specification limits.

distractors.text: Stability is about meeting spec limits; capability is about predictability over time
distractors.trap: Reverses the two: meeting spec limits is capability, predictability over time is stability.

distractors.text: Both terms mean the process has zero variation
distractors.trap: Neither implies zero variation; stable processes still have common-cause variation.

distractors.text: Stability concerns cost variance; capability concerns schedule variance
distractors.trap: Both are quality concepts about variation, not earned value cost or schedule variances.

explanation: Process stability = predictable, in-control variation over time. Process capability = the ability to meet specification limits. A process can be stable yet not capable.

eco_task: Plan and manage quality of products and deliverables
prompts: A histogram and a bar chart look similar. What distinguishes a histogram?

correct.text: It shows the frequency distribution of continuous numerical data grouped into intervals
correct.why: A histogram displays how often values fall within numerical intervals, showing the distribution of continuous data.

distractors.text: It compares discrete unrelated categories side by side
distractors.trap: Comparing discrete categories describes a bar chart, not a histogram.

distractors.text: It tracks a single variable over time against control limits
distractors.trap: That describes a control chart, not a histogram.

distractors.text: It ranks causes from most to least significant
distractors.trap: Ranking causes describes a Pareto chart, not a histogram.

explanation: A histogram shows the distribution of continuous data across intervals; a bar chart compares discrete categories.

prompts: Avoid is the threat strategy that eliminates the risk entirely. Which opportunity strategy is its mirror image?

correct.why: Exploit is the opportunity mirror of Avoid - it makes the upside certain, just as Avoid makes the downside impossible.

distractors.trap: Enhance only increases the probability or impact of an opportunity; it mirrors Mitigate, not Avoid.
Share hands the opportunity to a partner best able to capture it; it mirrors Transfer, not Avoid.
Accept takes no proactive action; it is the same name on both sides and is not the mirror of Avoid.

explanation: The aggressive pair is Avoid/Exploit: Avoid drives a threat probability to zero, Exploit drives an opportunity probability to certainty.

prompts: Mitigate reduces the probability or impact of a threat. Which opportunity strategy is the direct mirror of Mitigate?

correct.why: Enhance increases the probability or impact of an opportunity - the positive counterpart of reducing a threat.

distractors.trap: Exploit makes the opportunity certain; it mirrors Avoid, which eliminates a threat, not Mitigate.
Share assigns the opportunity to a third party; it mirrors Transfer, not Mitigate.
Transfer is itself a threat strategy, not an opportunity strategy, so it cannot be the mirror.

explanation: Mitigate (reduce threat P/I) mirrors Enhance (increase opportunity P/I). Both adjust probability or impact without forcing the outcome.

prompts: Transfer shifts the impact of a threat to a third party. Which opportunity strategy is its mirror?

correct.why: Share allocates ownership of an opportunity to a third party best able to capture the benefit - the positive counterpart of Transfer.

distractors.trap: Exploit makes the opportunity certain and is usually done within the team; it mirrors Avoid, not Transfer.
Enhance raises probability or impact; it mirrors Mitigate, not Transfer.
Accept takes no proactive action and applies to both sides; it is not the third-party mirror of Transfer.

explanation: Transfer/Share are the third-party pair: Transfer moves threat impact out, Share brings in a partner to realise an opportunity.

prompts: Which TWO risk response strategies apply to BOTH threats and opportunities? (Select 2)

correct.why: Acceptance applies to both: passively or actively acknowledging a threat or an opportunity without changing the plan to eliminate or ensure it.
Escalation applies to both: raising a threat or an opportunity that is outside the team's authority to a higher level.

distractors.trap: Mitigate applies only to threats - reducing probability or impact of a negative risk.
Exploit applies only to opportunities - ensuring a positive risk occurs.
Avoid applies only to threats - eliminating a negative risk.

explanation: Accept and Escalate are the two strategies shared by threats and opportunities. The other four come in threat/opportunity pairs.

prompts: Which of the following is NOT a positive (opportunity) risk response strategy?

correct.why: Mitigate is a threat strategy - it reduces the probability or impact of a negative risk, not a positive one.

distractors.trap: Exploit is an opportunity strategy that makes a beneficial event certain.
Enhance is an opportunity strategy that increases the probability or impact of a positive risk.
Share is an opportunity strategy that assigns the upside to a partner best able to realise it.

explanation: The opportunity strategies are Exploit, Enhance, Share (plus Accept/Escalate). Mitigate is a threat-only strategy.

prompts: A project buys an insurance policy so that the financial impact of a possible flood is borne by the insurer. Which threat response is this?

correct.why: Insurance is the classic Transfer: ownership of the impact is shifted to a third party, usually for a premium.

distractors.trap: Mitigate would reduce the flood's probability or impact directly (for example, raising equipment) rather than shifting the impact to another party.
Avoid would change the plan so the flood threat no longer applies, such as relocating the site, not paying a third party to carry the impact.
Accept would mean taking no proactive action and absorbing any loss, the opposite of buying insurance.

explanation: Buying insurance, warranties, or performance bonds shifts impact ownership to a third party - the definition of Transfer.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes Avoid from Mitigate?

correct.text: Avoid changes the plan so the threat no longer applies; Mitigate reduces the threat's probability or impact without removing it
correct.why: Avoid eliminates the exposure entirely; Mitigate only shrinks the probability or impact, leaving residual exposure.

distractors.text: Avoid reduces probability or impact; Mitigate shifts the impact to a third party
distractors.trap: This swaps the meanings - reducing P/I is Mitigate and shifting impact is Transfer; neither defines Avoid.

distractors.text: Avoid is used only for opportunities; Mitigate is used only for threats
distractors.trap: Both Avoid and Mitigate are threat strategies; neither is an opportunity strategy.

distractors.text: Avoid sets aside reserve to act if the risk occurs; Mitigate takes no action
distractors.trap: Setting aside reserve to act on occurrence describes active acceptance, not Avoid; Mitigate is proactive.

explanation: Avoid removes the threat or the team's exposure to it; Mitigate lowers probability or impact but exposure remains.

prompts: A team reroutes the project to drop a high-risk vendor entirely so a supply threat simply cannot occur. Which response strategy is this?

correct.why: Eliminating the source so the threat can no longer occur is Avoid.

distractors.trap: Mitigate would keep the vendor but reduce the chance or impact of a supply failure, not remove the vendor entirely.
Transfer would keep the threat but shift its impact to a third party, such as a penalty clause.
Accept would keep the vendor and take no proactive action, planning only to react if a failure happens.

explanation: Changing the plan so the threat no longer applies - dropping the risky vendor - is the textbook Avoid response.

prompts: Both Exploit and Enhance are opportunity strategies. Which one is correct when the team wants to make the opportunity certain to happen rather than just more likely?

correct.why: Exploit removes the uncertainty and ensures the opportunity is realised (probability driven to certainty).

distractors.trap: Enhance only increases the probability or impact of the opportunity; it does not guarantee it occurs.
Share allocates the opportunity to a third party to realise the benefit; it does not by itself make the opportunity certain.
Accept takes no proactive action to capture the opportunity at all.

explanation: Exploit makes the upside certain; Enhance merely improves its odds or size. This is the opportunity-side analogue of Avoid vs Mitigate.

prompts: A vendor could deliver a breakthrough feature early, but only a specialist partner can realise it. The team assigns that upside to the partner to jointly capture the benefit. Which opportunity response is this?

correct.why: Allocating ownership of an opportunity to the third party best able to capture the benefit is Share.

distractors.trap: Transfer is the threat-side analogue; it moves negative impact to a third party, not positive benefit.
Exploit would have the team itself ensure the opportunity occurs, not hand it to a partner.
Enhance increases the probability or impact but keeps ownership with the team rather than allocating it to a partner.

explanation: Share is the opportunity strategy of giving ownership to a partner best positioned to realise the benefit - the mirror of Transfer.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes active acceptance from passive acceptance of a risk?

correct.text: Active acceptance establishes a contingency reserve to use if the risk occurs; passive acceptance documents the risk and takes no action unless it happens
correct.why: Active acceptance prepares resources (reserve) in advance; passive acceptance simply acknowledges the risk and reacts only if needed.

distractors.text: Active acceptance eliminates the risk; passive acceptance reduces its impact
distractors.trap: Eliminating is Avoid and reducing is Mitigate; neither is a form of acceptance.

distractors.text: Active acceptance transfers the risk to a third party; passive acceptance keeps it in-house
distractors.trap: Transferring to a third party is Transfer, not active acceptance.

distractors.text: Active acceptance is only for opportunities; passive acceptance is only for threats
distractors.trap: Acceptance applies to both threats and opportunities; the active/passive split is about reserve, not sign.

explanation: The only difference is preparation: active acceptance sets up a contingency reserve; passive acceptance just acknowledges the risk and deals with it if it occurs.

prompts: A team decides not to change its plan for a minor risk, but sets aside a contingency reserve and will act only if the risk actually occurs. Which response is this?

correct.why: Setting aside a reserve to draw on if the risk materialises, while otherwise leaving the plan unchanged, is active acceptance.

distractors.trap: Passive acceptance would document the risk but establish no reserve or advance plan.
Mitigate would take proactive action now to reduce the risk's probability or impact, not just wait with a reserve.
A contingency plan is the predefined set of actions to execute on a trigger; here the response is the acceptance decision, supported by a reserve.

explanation: Active acceptance = acknowledge the risk plus hold a contingency reserve to act on if it occurs. Passive acceptance holds no reserve.

prompts: A risk is logged on the watch list with no reserve and no planned action; the team will simply deal with it if it ever occurs. Which response best describes this?

correct.why: Documenting a low-priority risk and taking no proactive action or reserve is passive acceptance.

distractors.trap: Avoid changes the plan to eliminate the threat; here the plan is unchanged.
Active acceptance would set aside a contingency reserve; this risk has none.
Mitigate would take action now to lower probability or impact; nothing proactive is being done here.

explanation: Low-priority risks placed on a watch list with no action are typically passively accepted. Adding a reserve would make it active acceptance.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a secondary risk from a residual risk?

correct.text: A secondary risk is a new risk created by implementing a response; a residual risk is what remains after the response is implemented
correct.why: Secondary = caused by the response; residual = leftover the response did not remove.

distractors.text: A secondary risk remains after the response; a residual risk is created by the response
distractors.trap: This reverses the definitions - residual remains, secondary is newly created.

distractors.text: A secondary risk is a lower-priority risk; a residual risk is a higher-priority risk
distractors.trap: Neither term refers to priority; both refer to timing relative to a response.

distractors.text: A secondary risk is an opportunity; a residual risk is a threat
distractors.trap: Both can be threats or opportunities; the distinction is origin, not sign.

explanation: Implementing a response can leave some risk behind (residual) and can spawn a brand-new risk (secondary).

prompts: After a response is implemented, the leftover exposure that the response did not fully eliminate is called what?

correct.why: Residual risk is the portion of risk that remains after responses have been applied.

distractors.trap: Secondary risk is a new risk created by the response, not the leftover of the original risk.
Overall project risk is the aggregate effect of uncertainty on the whole project, not the remainder of one risk after response.
A trigger is the signal that a risk is occurring, not the leftover exposure after a response.

explanation: Residual risk = what is left over after the planned response is in place. It is often accepted if within thresholds.

prompts: To mitigate a schedule threat the team hires a new subcontractor; that subcontractor's unproven reliability now becomes a fresh risk of its own. What is this newly created risk called?

correct.why: A risk that arises as a direct result of implementing a risk response is a secondary risk.

distractors.trap: Residual risk is the leftover of the original schedule threat after mitigation, not the new subcontractor risk.
An issue has already occurred; the subcontractor reliability is still an uncertain future event.
There is no such category; the backup plan itself is a fallback plan, not a fallback risk.

explanation: Responses can generate new risks. The subcontractor reliability is a secondary risk born from the mitigation action.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes contingency reserve from management reserve?

correct.text: Contingency reserve covers identified risks and is inside the cost/schedule baseline; management reserve covers unforeseen work and sits outside the baseline
correct.why: Contingency = known-unknowns, in baseline, PM-controlled; management = unknown-unknowns, outside baseline, management-controlled.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve covers unforeseen work outside the baseline; management reserve covers identified risks inside the baseline
distractors.trap: This reverses them - contingency is for identified risks inside the baseline; management reserve is outside.

distractors.text: Both reserves are part of the cost baseline and both cover identified risks
distractors.trap: Only contingency reserve is in the baseline for identified risks; management reserve is outside the baseline for unknown-unknowns.

distractors.text: Contingency reserve requires management approval to use; management reserve is controlled by the project manager
distractors.trap: It is the opposite - the PM controls contingency reserve; using management reserve typically needs management approval.

explanation: Contingency reserve = identified risks (known-unknowns), inside the performance baseline. Management reserve = unforeseen work (unknown-unknowns), outside the baseline.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A budget amount is held for unknown-unknowns - work within project scope that could not be foreseen - and is not part of the performance measurement baseline. Which reserve is this?

correct.why: Management reserve is held outside the baseline for unforeseen work (unknown-unknowns) within scope.

distractors.trap: Contingency reserve is for identified risks with response strategies and is inside the cost or schedule baseline.
The cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget that excludes management reserve, not a reserve itself.
A buffer is a protective allowance embedded in flow or timing, typically for variability, not the management reserve for unknown-unknowns.

explanation: Unknown-unknowns outside the baseline = management reserve. Using it usually requires a baseline change and management approval.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Time or money set aside specifically for identified risks that have active response strategies, included within the cost or schedule baseline, is called what?

correct.why: Contingency reserve covers known, identified risks with planned responses and lives inside the baseline.

distractors.trap: Management reserve is for unforeseen (unidentified) work and sits outside the baseline.
A fallback plan is a backup course of action, not a budget or schedule reserve.
Funding requirements describe the timing and amounts of funding needed, not a reserve for identified risks.

explanation: Identified risks + active responses + inside the baseline = contingency reserve.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a contingency plan from a fallback plan?

correct.text: A contingency plan is the planned response executed when a defined risk trigger occurs; a fallback plan is the backup used if that primary response fails or is insufficient
correct.why: Contingency = first planned response on a trigger; fallback = the Plan B if the contingency does not work.

distractors.text: A contingency plan is the backup if the primary response fails; a fallback plan is the first response
distractors.trap: This reverses them - the contingency plan is the first planned response; the fallback is the backup.

distractors.text: A contingency plan is money set aside; a fallback plan is time set aside
distractors.trap: Money or time set aside is the contingency reserve; a contingency plan is a set of actions, not funds.

distractors.text: A contingency plan applies to opportunities; a fallback plan applies to threats
distractors.trap: Both are responses to risk events and are not distinguished by opportunity versus threat.

explanation: Contingency plan = the primary planned response triggered by a risk; fallback plan = the secondary plan if the primary one fails.

prompts: A team prepares a Plan B to use only if its primary risk response turns out to be ineffective. What is this Plan B called?

correct.why: A fallback plan is the backup course of action used if the primary response proves insufficient or a specific risk still occurs.

distractors.trap: The contingency plan is the primary planned response to the risk event, not the backup if that response fails.
A workaround is an unplanned response improvised for an unidentified risk or issue, not a pre-prepared Plan B.
Mitigation reduces probability or impact proactively; it is not the backup-if-primary-fails plan.

explanation: A fallback plan is the pre-planned backup for when the primary (contingency) response does not work.

prompts: A contingency plan and a contingency reserve are often confused. Which pairing is correct?

correct.text: The contingency plan is the set of actions to take; the contingency reserve is the time or money set aside to support those actions
correct.why: The plan is what you do; the reserve is the resource that funds doing it.

distractors.text: The contingency plan is the money set aside; the contingency reserve is the list of actions
distractors.trap: Reversed - the reserve is the money/time; the plan is the actions.

distractors.text: Both are amounts of budget held outside the baseline
distractors.trap: Neither is outside the baseline as a pair; the contingency reserve is inside the baseline and the plan is not a budget at all.

distractors.text: Both refer to the same thing under different names
distractors.trap: They are distinct: one is an action plan, the other a resource allowance.

explanation: Contingency plan = the actions; contingency reserve = the time/money that backs them.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a risk from an issue?

correct.text: A risk is an uncertain future event that may or may not occur; an issue is a condition that already exists and needs management now
correct.why: Risk = uncertain, future, may not happen; issue = present, certain, happening now.

distractors.text: A risk has already occurred; an issue may occur in the future
distractors.trap: This reverses them - the issue is current and the risk is the future uncertainty.

distractors.text: A risk always has a negative effect; an issue always has a positive effect
distractors.trap: Risks can be threats or opportunities, and issues are generally negative conditions; effect sign is not the distinction.

distractors.text: A risk is recorded in the issue log; an issue is recorded in the risk register
distractors.trap: Reversed - risks go in the risk register and issues go in the issue log.

explanation: The key is timing and certainty: a risk is uncertain and in the future; an issue has already materialised.

prompts: A supplier has just announced it cannot deliver a critical part next week; the team must respond immediately. In risk terms, this is now best classified as what?

correct.why: A condition that has already occurred and requires immediate management is an issue, not a risk.

distractors.trap: A risk is still uncertain; here the event has already happened, so it is no longer a risk.
A trigger signals that a risk is about to occur; the event here has already occurred, making it an issue.
A secondary risk is a new risk created by a response; this is a materialised problem, i.e., an issue.

explanation: Once an uncertain event actually occurs, it ceases to be a risk and becomes an issue requiring current action.

prompts: A hazard and a risk are sometimes used interchangeably. Which statement is correct?

correct.text: A hazard is a source or condition that can cause harm; a risk is the uncertain event or condition and its effect on objectives
correct.why: The hazard is the potential source of harm; the risk is the uncertain event and its effect.

distractors.text: A hazard is the uncertain event; a risk is the source of harm
distractors.trap: Reversed - the source of harm is the hazard; the uncertain event and effect is the risk.

distractors.text: A hazard is always an opportunity; a risk is always a threat
distractors.trap: A hazard is a source of potential harm and is not an opportunity; risks may be threats or opportunities.

distractors.text: A hazard has already occurred; a risk has not
distractors.trap: A hazard is a standing source/condition, not a materialised event; that timing distinction describes issue versus risk.

explanation: A hazard is a source of potential harm (for example, a chemical store); the risk is the uncertain event arising from it and its effect on objectives.

eco_task: Plan and manage uncertainty and ambiguity
prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes ambiguity risk from uncertainty?

correct.text: Ambiguity is about unclear meaning or multiple possible interpretations; uncertainty is about not knowing what will happen
correct.why: Ambiguity = lack of clarity/multiple readings; uncertainty = lack of knowledge about the outcome.

distractors.text: Ambiguity is the rate of change; uncertainty is the lack of clarity
distractors.trap: Rate and turbulence of change is volatility, not ambiguity.

distractors.text: Ambiguity is a known event; uncertainty is a documented assumption
distractors.trap: Neither maps this way; ambiguity is about unclear interpretation, not a known event.

distractors.text: Ambiguity applies only to cost; uncertainty applies only to schedule
distractors.trap: Both apply across objectives; they are not split by cost versus schedule.

explanation: Ambiguity is conceptual unclearness or competing interpretations; uncertainty is simply not knowing the outcome.

eco_task: Plan and manage uncertainty and ambiguity
prompts: A market is changing so rapidly and turbulently that conditions shift faster than the plan can absorb. Which characteristic of project uncertainty best names this?

correct.why: Volatility is the speed and turbulence of change that can outpace the plan.

distractors.trap: Ambiguity is about unclear meaning or multiple interpretations, not the rate of change.
Complexity arises from many interacting parts and emergent behaviour, not specifically the speed of change.
Residual risk is what remains after a response is applied, unrelated to the rate of environmental change.

explanation: Volatility names rapid, turbulent change (the V in VUCA). Ambiguity, complexity, and uncertainty describe different facets.

eco_task: Plan and manage uncertainty and ambiguity
prompts: Stakeholders interpret a key requirement in several conflicting ways and the true intent is unclear. Which type of project uncertainty is this best described as?

correct.why: Multiple conflicting interpretations and unclear meaning are the hallmark of ambiguity.

distractors.trap: Volatility concerns rapid change over time, not unclear or conflicting meaning.
Variability concerns the spread of possible measurable outcomes, not unclear interpretation of meaning.
Uncertainty is the broad state of not knowing the outcome; ambiguity is the more specific case of unclear or multiple meanings.

explanation: Conflicting interpretations and lack of conceptual clarity = ambiguity. Variability is about spread of outcomes; volatility is about rate of change.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes risk appetite from risk threshold?

correct.text: Risk appetite is the general willingness to accept uncertainty in pursuit of reward; risk threshold is the measurable boundary of acceptable variation that expresses it
correct.why: Appetite = broad attitude; threshold = the quantified limit that operationalises that attitude.

distractors.text: Risk appetite is the measurable limit; risk threshold is the general attitude
distractors.trap: Reversed - the measurable limit is the threshold; the broad attitude is the appetite.

distractors.text: Risk appetite applies to threats only; risk threshold applies to opportunities only
distractors.trap: Both concepts span threats and opportunities; they are not split by sign.

distractors.text: Risk appetite is set by the project team; risk threshold is set by regulators
distractors.trap: Neither is defined by who sets it in this way; the distinction is attitude versus measurable boundary.

explanation: Appetite is the qualitative willingness to take on uncertainty; the threshold is the measurable boundary that makes that willingness actionable.

prompts: The steering committee states that any schedule slip beyond 10 working days must be escalated. The 10-day figure is an example of what?

correct.why: A measurable boundary of acceptable variation that triggers escalation or action is a risk threshold.

distractors.trap: Risk appetite is the broad, qualitative willingness to accept uncertainty, not a specific measurable boundary.
Risk tolerance is the degree of variation a stakeholder can withstand; the explicit 10-day boundary that expresses it is the threshold.
A trigger is the event indicating a risk is occurring; the 10-day figure is the predefined limit, not the event itself.

explanation: A specific, measurable limit (10 days) is a risk threshold - the quantified expression of appetite and tolerance.

prompts: Risk appetite and risk tolerance are closely related. Which pairing is correct?

correct.text: Risk appetite is the broad willingness to accept uncertainty in pursuit of value; risk tolerance is the specific degree of variation a stakeholder can withstand
correct.why: Appetite is the overarching attitude; tolerance is the bounded amount of variation that can be lived with.

distractors.text: Risk appetite is the measurable boundary; risk tolerance is the qualitative attitude
distractors.trap: The measurable boundary is the threshold; appetite is qualitative and tolerance is the withstandable variation.

distractors.text: Risk appetite and risk tolerance are identical terms
distractors.trap: They are related but distinct - appetite is the broad willingness, tolerance is the withstandable degree of variation.

distractors.text: Risk appetite is about opportunities; risk tolerance is about threats
distractors.trap: Both span threats and opportunities; they are not divided by sign.

explanation: Appetite (broad willingness) and tolerance (degree of variation withstood) are related but distinct; the threshold is the measurable line.

prompts: A threat has a 30 percent probability of occurring and would cost 100,000 if it does. What is its expected monetary value (EMV)?

correct.why: EMV = probability times impact = 0.30 times 100,000 = 30,000, expressed as a negative because it is a threat (cost).

distractors.trap: 100,000 is the full impact if the risk occurs, not the probability-weighted EMV.
The magnitude 30,000 is right but the sign is wrong; threats yield a negative EMV.
70,000 reflects 0.70 times 100,000 (the probability of NOT occurring), which is not how EMV is computed.

explanation: EMV multiplies probability by impact. For a threat the result is negative: 0.30 times 100,000 = minus 30,000.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes expected monetary value (EMV) from decision tree analysis?

correct.text: EMV is the probability-weighted value calculation of an uncertain outcome; decision tree analysis is the broader model that structures decisions and chance events and often uses EMV within its branches
correct.why: EMV is the per-outcome calculation; the decision tree is the diagram that organises decisions, chances, and EMVs to choose a path.

distractors.text: EMV is a diagram of decision branches; decision tree analysis is a single number
distractors.trap: Reversed - EMV is the calculation/number; the decision tree is the diagram.

distractors.text: EMV is a qualitative technique; decision tree analysis is a brainstorming technique
distractors.trap: Both are quantitative; neither is brainstorming, and EMV is numeric not qualitative.

distractors.text: EMV applies only to threats; decision tree analysis applies only to opportunities
distractors.trap: Both handle threats and opportunities and are not split by sign.

explanation: EMV is the probability-weighted figure for an outcome; decision tree analysis is the structured model that often computes EMV along each branch to choose the best decision.

prompts: A team needs to choose between two designs whose payoffs depend on uncertain test results. They diagram the decision node, the chance branches with probabilities, and the payoff at each end, then compute the best path. Which technique is this?

correct.why: Mapping a decision node, probabilistic chance branches, and end payoffs to choose the best path is decision tree analysis.

distractors.trap: Sensitivity analysis identifies which variables most affect the outcome, usually via a tornado diagram, not a branch-and-payoff decision model.
Monte Carlo runs many iterations to produce an outcome distribution; it does not structure a single discrete decision with branches and payoffs.
The P-I matrix is a qualitative prioritisation grid, not a quantitative branch-and-payoff decision model.

explanation: Decision trees model discrete decisions plus chance events and payoffs, typically using EMV on each branch to select the optimal choice.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis from Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis?

correct.text: Qualitative analysis prioritises individual risks using relative probability and impact assessment; quantitative analysis numerically models the combined effect of risks on overall objectives
correct.why: Qualitative = relative prioritisation (often a P-I matrix); quantitative = numeric modelling of combined effects.

distractors.text: Qualitative analysis numerically models combined outcomes; quantitative analysis ranks risks on a relative scale
distractors.trap: Reversed - numeric modelling is quantitative; relative ranking is qualitative.

distractors.text: Qualitative analysis is performed only after quantitative analysis
distractors.trap: Qualitative analysis generally precedes quantitative analysis, which is applied to the highest-priority risks.

distractors.text: Qualitative analysis applies only to opportunities; quantitative analysis applies only to threats
distractors.trap: Both processes handle threats and opportunities and are not split by sign.

explanation: Qualitative prioritises risks subjectively (probability and impact); quantitative models their numeric, combined effect on objectives.

prompts: A project manager wants to know which uncertain inputs - duration, cost rate, or scope volume - drive the project outcome the most, displayed on a tornado diagram. Which technique is this?

correct.why: Ranking which variables most affect the outcome, displayed as a tornado diagram, is sensitivity analysis.

distractors.trap: Monte Carlo estimates the probability distribution of combined outcomes; it does not by itself rank single variables on a tornado diagram.
Decision tree analysis structures discrete decisions and chance branches, not a ranking of input variables by influence.
EMV computes a probability-weighted value, not a ranking of which variables matter most.

explanation: Identifying the most influential variables (tornado diagram) is the signature use of sensitivity analysis.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a risk breakdown structure (RBS) from a work breakdown structure (WBS)?

correct.text: An RBS is a hierarchy of potential sources of risk organised by risk category; a WBS is a hierarchy of the project scope decomposed into deliverables and work packages
correct.why: RBS organises risk sources by category; WBS decomposes the deliverable scope of the work.

distractors.text: An RBS decomposes project deliverables; a WBS organises risks by category
distractors.trap: Reversed - the WBS decomposes deliverables and the RBS organises risk sources.

distractors.text: An RBS lists detailed individual risks with owners and responses; a WBS lists project activities
distractors.trap: Detailed individual risks with owners live in the risk register, not the RBS; the RBS is a categorical hierarchy.

distractors.text: An RBS and a WBS are the same structure used for different reports
distractors.trap: They are different structures - one for risk sources, one for scope decomposition.

explanation: The RBS is a categorical hierarchy of risk sources; the WBS is a deliverable-oriented decomposition of project scope.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes the risk register from the risk report?

correct.text: The risk register records detailed information about individual risks; the risk report summarises overall project risk and aggregated individual-risk information
correct.why: Register = granular per-risk detail; report = the summary of overall and aggregated risk.

distractors.text: The risk register summarises overall project risk; the risk report lists each individual risk in detail
distractors.trap: Reversed - detail per risk is the register; overall/aggregated summary is the report.

distractors.text: The risk register is a hierarchy of risk categories; the risk report lists work packages
distractors.trap: A hierarchy of risk categories is the RBS; work packages belong to the WBS, not the risk report.

distractors.text: The risk register exists only in predictive projects; the risk report only in agile
distractors.trap: Both artefacts can be used across approaches; they are not split by development approach.

explanation: The register holds detailed, individual risk entries; the report summarises overall project risk and aggregated information.

prompts: An event or condition that signals a risk is about to occur or has just occurred, prompting the team to launch its planned response, is called what?

correct.why: A trigger is the warning sign that a risk is materialising and that a response should be initiated.

distractors.trap: A threshold is the predefined limit of acceptable variation, not the event that signals a risk is occurring.
A secondary risk is a new risk created by a response, not the signal that a risk is occurring.
An issue is a problem that has fully materialised and needs management; a trigger is the early signal that prompts the planned response.

explanation: A trigger (or trigger condition) is the signal that a risk is occurring and that the contingency response should start.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a risk owner from a risk action owner?

correct.text: The risk owner is accountable for monitoring a specific risk and ensuring the response; the risk action owner carries out the specific approved response actions
correct.why: Owner = accountable for the risk; action owner = executes the assigned response task.

distractors.text: The risk owner carries out response tasks; the risk action owner is accountable for the risk
distractors.trap: Reversed - accountability for the risk sits with the risk owner; execution of actions sits with the action owner.

distractors.text: The risk owner exists only for threats; the risk action owner only for opportunities
distractors.trap: Both roles apply to threats and opportunities and are not split by sign.

distractors.text: The risk owner is always the project manager; the risk action owner is always the sponsor
distractors.trap: Either role can be assigned to various stakeholders; they are not fixed to the PM or sponsor.

explanation: The risk owner is accountable for the risk and its response; the risk action owner is the person who performs a specific response action.

prompts: An unidentified risk suddenly materialises and the team improvises an unplanned response on the spot. What is this response called?

correct.why: An unplanned response to a risk that was not previously identified, or to a materialised issue, is a workaround.

distractors.trap: A contingency plan is a predefined response to an identified risk; here the risk was never identified, so the response is improvised.
A fallback plan is a pre-prepared backup; a workaround is improvised because nothing was prepared.
Mitigation is a proactive, planned action taken before a risk occurs, not an improvised reaction afterward.

explanation: A workaround is an unplanned, reactive response to an unidentified risk or a current issue - distinct from the pre-planned contingency and fallback plans.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a risk audit from a risk review?

correct.text: A risk audit examines the effectiveness of risk responses and of the risk management process itself; a risk review is a recurring check of current risks, their status, and response effectiveness
correct.why: Audit = evaluate the process and response effectiveness; review = recurring status check on the risks themselves.

distractors.text: A risk audit is a recurring status check of current risks; a risk review evaluates the risk process
distractors.trap: Reversed - the recurring status check is the review; evaluating the process is the audit.

distractors.text: A risk audit only counts open risks; a risk review only counts closed risks
distractors.trap: Neither is a simple count of open or closed risks; both assess status and effectiveness.

distractors.text: A risk audit is performed by the project team; a risk review is performed by external regulators
distractors.trap: Neither is defined by who performs it in this way; the distinction is process effectiveness versus recurring status.

explanation: A risk audit evaluates how well responses and the overall risk process are working; a risk review is the recurring examination of current risks and their status.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes an individual project risk from overall project risk?

correct.text: An individual project risk is a single uncertain event or condition affecting objectives; overall project risk is the aggregate effect of all uncertainty on the project as a whole
correct.why: Individual = one discrete risk; overall = the cumulative effect of all uncertainty on the project.

distractors.text: An individual project risk is the aggregate of all uncertainty; overall project risk is a single event
distractors.trap: Reversed - the single event is the individual risk; the aggregate is overall project risk.

distractors.text: An individual project risk is always a threat; overall project risk is always an opportunity
distractors.trap: Both individual and overall risk can be positive or negative; they are not divided by sign.

distractors.text: An individual project risk is recorded only in the risk report; overall project risk only in the register
distractors.trap: Individual risks are detailed in the register; overall project risk is summarised in the report - this option reverses them.

explanation: Individual project risk is one discrete uncertainty; overall project risk is the combined effect of all sources of uncertainty on the whole project.

prompts: An activity can slip three days without delaying the early start of any successor. Which scheduling measure does this describe?

correct.why: Free float is the time an activity can be delayed without delaying the early start of any successor activity.

distractors.trap: Total float protects the project finish or a constraint, not specifically the successor early start.
Lag is an intentional waiting time between activities, not a measure of available slip.
Plausible-sounding term, but the value tied to a successor early start is free float.

explanation: Free float relates to the next activity; total float relates to the whole project. The successor early start is the tell for free float.

prompts: Which resource optimization technique may change the critical path and the project finish date when it resolves an over-allocation?

correct.why: Leveling adjusts start and finish dates to resolve over-allocation even if that shifts the critical path or delays completion.

distractors.trap: Smoothing only moves activities within their available float, so it preserves the finish date and critical path.
Fast tracking is schedule compression by overlapping work, not a resource optimization technique.
Not a standard technique name; the float-bound technique is resource smoothing, which does not move the critical path.

explanation: Leveling can move the critical path because it ignores float limits to fix over-allocation; smoothing stays within float and keeps the finish date.
