prompts: A manager shortens the schedule by adding resources to critical activities, accepting higher cost. Which technique is this?

correct.why: Crashing shortens duration for the least incremental cost by adding resources to critical activities.

distractors.trap: Fast tracking compresses by overlapping activities, which adds risk rather than cost.
Leveling balances resource demand and usually lengthens, not shortens, the schedule.
Adding lead overlaps activities; it does not add resources to buy time.

explanation: Crashing trades money for time; fast tracking trades risk for time. Adding resources to buy duration is the signature of crashing.

prompts: The team overlaps design and build phases that were planned in sequence to save time. What is the primary downside they accept?

correct.why: Fast tracking overlaps sequential work, so later changes in the predecessor can force rework in the overlapped successor.

distractors.trap: That is the trade-off of crashing, where resources are added, not fast tracking.
Compression techniques shorten the schedule without changing scope.

distractors.text: Loss of total float on every path
distractors.trap: Overlapping affects the overlapped path, not float on every path uniformly.

explanation: Fast tracking buys time by overlapping; its cost is added risk and potential rework, distinguishing it from crashing.

prompts: Which scheduling method explicitly accounts for resource constraints and protects delivery with buffers placed around the work that governs completion?

correct.why: Critical chain plans around resource constraints and uses feeding and project buffers to protect the delivery date.

distractors.trap: CPM focuses on logical dependencies and float, not on resource constraints or buffers.
PDM is a network diagramming notation, not a buffer-based scheduling method.
Rolling wave is a progressive detailing technique, unrelated to resource buffers.

explanation: Buffers plus resource constraints signal critical chain; logic and float alone signal critical path.

prompts: In which logical relationship can a successor activity not start until its predecessor has finished?

correct.why: In finish-to-start the predecessor must finish before the successor may start, the most common relationship.

distractors.trap: Start-to-start only requires the predecessor to start before the successor starts.
Finish-to-finish governs when the successor may finish, not when it may start.
Start-to-finish requires the predecessor to start before the successor finishes, a rare relationship.

explanation: Finish-to-start is the default and most common precedence relationship; the others change which endpoints are constrained.

prompts: Documentation drafting can begin as soon as coding begins, and both proceed together. Which logical relationship best models this?

correct.why: Start-to-start lets the successor begin once the predecessor has begun, allowing parallel progress.

distractors.trap: Finish-to-start would force documentation to wait until coding is fully complete.
Finish-to-finish ties the finishes together, not the starts.
Start-to-finish constrains the successor finish to the predecessor start, which does not match parallel starts.

explanation: When two activities should kick off together, the start of one gates the start of the other, which is start-to-start.

prompts: A new accounting system cannot be shut off until the replacement system is up and running. Which relationship type captures this rare case?

correct.why: Start-to-finish requires the predecessor to start before the successor can finish, modeling a handover where the old system ends only once the new one begins.

distractors.trap: Finish-to-start would require the new system to fully finish before the old one ends, which is not the handover described.
Finish-to-finish ties both finishes together, not a start to a finish.
Start-to-start ties the two starts, not the predecessor start to the successor finish.

explanation: Start-to-finish is the rare relationship used in handover or shift scenarios where a successor can finish only after the predecessor starts.

prompts: A planner advances a successor so it starts before its predecessor finishes. What has been applied?

correct.why: A lead advances the successor relative to the predecessor, allowing overlap.

distractors.trap: Lag delays the successor relative to the predecessor; it is the opposite of lead.
Float is available slack in the schedule, not a deliberate acceleration of a successor.
A buffer is protective reserve time, not an overlap added to a relationship.

explanation: Lead accelerates the successor and lag delays it; the question describes acceleration, so it is lead.

prompts: Concrete must cure for seven days before the next activity can begin. Which scheduling element models this required wait?

correct.why: Lag inserts a required waiting time between a predecessor and successor, such as a curing period.

distractors.trap: Lead overlaps activities to start sooner, the opposite of a required wait.
Delay is an unplanned condition of lateness, while a curing period is intentionally modeled.
The required relationship may be mandatory, but the waiting time itself is modeled as lag.

explanation: A planned, required wait between activities is lag; lead does the opposite by overlapping them.

prompts: You must pour a foundation before erecting walls because physics requires it. What kind of dependency is this?

correct.why: A mandatory dependency is inherent in the nature of the work or required by contract, such as building physically requiring the foundation first.

distractors.trap: Discretionary dependencies reflect a preferred sequence the team chooses, not a physical requirement.
External dependencies involve non-project parties; pouring before walls is internal and physical.
It is internal, but the defining trait here is that it is mandatory due to the nature of the work.

explanation: Hard logic required by the work itself is a mandatory dependency; team-chosen ordering is discretionary.

prompts: A dependency based on best practices and a preferred sequence, rather than a physical or contractual requirement, is also known by which term?

correct.why: Discretionary dependencies are also called soft logic because they reflect preferred, changeable sequencing.

distractors.trap: Hard logic is the synonym for mandatory dependencies, which are required by the work.
External dependencies depend on non-project parties, not on preferred sequencing.
Lag is a waiting time, not a category of dependency.

explanation: Discretionary equals soft logic; mandatory equals hard logic. Preferred sequencing maps to soft logic.

prompts: Your project waits on a permit issued by a government agency outside the project team. How should this dependency be classified?

correct.why: An external dependency links a project activity to a non-project activity outside the team control, such as a regulatory permit.

distractors.trap: It may be required, but its defining trait is that the controlling party is outside the project.
Internal dependencies are between activities the team controls; a permit is not under team control.
Discretionary dependencies are team-chosen sequencing, not reliance on an outside party.

explanation: Reliance on a party outside the project boundary is an external dependency; the internal versus external split is about who controls the work.

prompts: Which schedule element is a zero-duration marker for a significant point or event rather than work that consumes time?

correct.why: A milestone is a significant point or event in the schedule with zero duration.

distractors.trap: An activity is scheduled work with duration, not a zero-duration marker.
A hammock is a summary activity that spans time, so it has duration.
A work package is the lowest level of planned work, not a point-in-time marker.

explanation: Milestones mark events and have no duration; activities and work packages represent work that takes time.

prompts: A team has weak data and chooses a three-point estimate that weights all three values equally. Which distribution have they used?

correct.why: The triangular distribution averages optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic with equal weight.

distractors.trap: The beta or PERT distribution weights the most likely value by four, not equally.
A normal distribution is symmetric around a mean and is not the standard three-point form.
A uniform distribution treats all values across a range as equally likely, not three specific points.

explanation: Equal weighting of the three points is triangular; four-times weighting of the most likely is beta or PERT.

prompts: Near-term work is planned in detail while work further out is kept at a high level until more is known. Which specific technique is this?

correct.why: Rolling wave planning details imminent work now and elaborates later work as information improves.

distractors.trap: Progressive elaboration is the broader concept of refining plans over time; rolling wave is the specific scheduling technique.
Decomposition breaks scope into smaller parts; it does not decide how much to plan now versus later.
Scope creep is uncontrolled growth of scope, not a planning technique.

explanation: Rolling wave is a specific application of the broader idea of progressive elaboration; the timing of detail is the tell.

prompts: In critical chain scheduling, what is placed where a non-critical feeding path joins the critical chain to protect it from upstream delay?

correct.why: A feeding buffer protects the critical chain at the point a feeding path merges into it.

distractors.trap: The project buffer sits at the end of the chain to protect the final delivery date, not the merge points.
Contingency reserve covers identified risks in the baseline; it is not the critical chain merge protection.
Management reserve covers unforeseen work outside the baseline, not a feeding path merge.

explanation: Critical chain uses feeding buffers at merge points and a project buffer at the end; the merge point is the feeding buffer.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Which reserve is held inside the cost or schedule baseline to handle identified risks that have active response strategies?

correct.why: Contingency reserve is inside the baseline and addresses known, identified risks.

distractors.trap: Management reserve sits outside the baseline and covers unknown unknowns, not identified risks.
A feeding buffer is a critical chain construct protecting the chain, not a baseline reserve for identified risks.
A project buffer is a critical chain end buffer, not the baseline reserve for known risks.

explanation: Contingency reserve is in the baseline for known risks; management reserve is outside it for unknown work.

prompts: Which critical path method calculation works from the project start to determine each activity early start and early finish?

correct.why: The forward pass moves from the start through the network to compute early start and early finish dates.

distractors.trap: The backward pass works from the end to compute late start and late finish dates.
Compression shortens duration; it does not compute early dates.
Reserve analysis sizes contingency and management reserves, not network dates.

explanation: Forward pass yields early dates from the start; backward pass yields late dates from the end.

prompts: Working backward from the project finish to find the latest each activity can start and finish without delaying the project is the result of which calculation?

correct.why: The backward pass computes late start and late finish dates by moving from the project end toward the start.

distractors.trap: The forward pass computes earliest, not latest, dates and moves from the start.
Critical chain adds resource constraints and buffers; it is not the late-date calculation itself.
Variance analysis compares planned to actual results, not network late dates.

explanation: Late dates come from the backward pass; subtracting early from late dates yields total float.

prompts: An activity has zero total float, so any delay to it pushes out the project finish. What is such an activity called?

correct.why: A critical activity has zero float and directly drives the project finish date if delayed.

distractors.trap: A near-critical activity has low but nonzero float, so it is not yet driving the finish.
A milestone is a zero-duration marker, not necessarily an activity with zero float.
A hammock is a summary activity spanning time, not defined by zero float.

explanation: Zero float on the longest path defines a critical activity; small positive float defines near-critical.

prompts: A practitioner uses the word slack while a PMI document uses a different word for the same general idea of schedule flexibility. Which PMI term matches slack?

correct.why: Float is the PMI term; slack is the informal practitioner synonym for the same scheduling flexibility.

distractors.trap: Lag is a deliberate waiting time, not the available flexibility that slack refers to.
A buffer is protective reserve in critical chain, not the PMI synonym for slack.
Lead is acceleration of a successor, not a measure of available slack.

explanation: Slack and float are synonyms for schedule flexibility; lead, lag, and buffer are different constructs.

prompts: Which artifact is the approved version of the schedule model used as the basis for comparison against actual results?

correct.why: The schedule baseline is the approved version of the schedule model used for performance comparison and change control.

distractors.trap: The schedule model is the working representation; only the approved version is the baseline.
The project schedule is the output of the model, not specifically the approved control reference.
A milestone list records significant events, not the approved baseline for comparison.

explanation: The schedule baseline is the approved, change-controlled reference; the schedule model is the editable working version.

prompts: Crashing and fast tracking are both examples of which broader category of techniques?

correct.why: Schedule compression is the broad family of techniques that shorten duration without reducing scope; crashing and fast tracking are members.

distractors.trap: Resource optimization covers leveling and smoothing, not crashing and fast tracking.
Network analysis computes paths and float; it is not the compression family.
Critical chain is a scheduling method with buffers, not the category that contains crashing and fast tracking.

explanation: Crashing and fast tracking are both schedule compression techniques; leveling and smoothing are resource optimization techniques.

prompts: Which estimating technique does NOT belong with the other three?

correct.why: Resource leveling is a schedule resource optimization technique, not a cost or duration estimating technique like the others.

distractors.trap: Analogous estimating is a genuine estimating technique using similar past work.
Parametric estimating is a genuine estimating technique using a statistical relationship.
Bottom-up estimating is a genuine estimating technique that aggregates component estimates.

explanation: Three options are estimating techniques; resource leveling is the odd one out as a scheduling technique.

prompts: Early in a project with little detail, a manager estimates cost using the actual cost of a similar past project as a quick reference. Which technique is this?

correct.why: Analogous estimating uses historical data from a similar activity or project for a quick top-down figure.

distractors.trap: Bottom-up estimating builds detail from component estimates, which is not possible with little detail.
Parametric estimating multiplies a unit rate by a quantity, not a whole-project comparison.
Three-point estimating uses optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values, not a single past project.

explanation: Comparison with one similar past project for a quick figure is analogous; component build-up is bottom-up; unit-rate math is parametric.

prompts: A planner estimates cabling cost as cost per meter multiplied by total meters required. Which estimating technique is being used?

correct.why: Parametric estimating applies a statistical unit rate to the quantity of work to derive the estimate.

distractors.trap: Analogous estimating compares to a similar whole project, not a unit-rate calculation.
Bottom-up aggregates detailed component estimates, not a single unit rate times quantity.
Reserve analysis sizes contingency, not the base estimate from a unit rate.

explanation: A unit rate multiplied by a quantity is the signature of parametric estimating.

prompts: A team estimates each lowest-level work package separately and rolls the estimates up into a total. Which technique gives the most detailed and usually most accurate result?

correct.why: Bottom-up estimating aggregates estimates from the lowest-level components, yielding high detail and accuracy.

distractors.trap: Analogous estimating is a quicker top-down comparison, generally less accurate than bottom-up.
Parametric uses unit rates; it is not the component-by-component roll-up described.
Rolling wave is a progressive planning technique, not a cost estimating method.

explanation: Estimating each component and summing is bottom-up; it is the most detailed but most effort-intensive approach.

prompts: A task needs 40 labor hours of work but is scheduled across five calendar days. Which term describes the five days of elapsed time?

correct.why: Duration is the elapsed number of work periods on the schedule, distinct from the labor content.

distractors.trap: Effort is the labor content, here the 40 hours, not the elapsed time.
Lag is a waiting time inserted between activities, not the elapsed time of the task itself.
Float is available slack before delay matters, not the elapsed time of the activity.

explanation: Duration is elapsed time; effort is labor units. The five calendar days are duration; the 40 hours are effort.

prompts: A path has only two days of float and the project manager watches it closely because exhausting that float would make it schedule-driving. What is this path called?

correct.why: A near-critical path has low float and can become critical if that float is consumed.

distractors.trap: The critical path already has zero float and drives the project duration now.
Not a standard path designation; the low-float path watched for becoming critical is near-critical.
A feeding path is a critical chain concept; the low-float watch path is the near-critical path.

explanation: Low but nonzero float that could become schedule-driving marks the near-critical path; zero float marks the critical path.

prompts: Hard logic is to mandatory dependency as soft logic is to which term?

correct.why: Soft logic is the synonym for a discretionary dependency, just as hard logic is the synonym for a mandatory one.

distractors.trap: External dependencies depend on outside parties; they are not the soft logic synonym.
Internal versus external is about control, not the mandatory versus discretionary mapping.
Mandatory dependency is hard logic, the opposite of soft logic.

explanation: Hard logic equals mandatory, soft logic equals discretionary; the relationship mirrors the synonym pairings.

prompts: A manager wants the full probability distribution of possible project completion dates, not just which variable matters most. Which technique should be used?

correct.why: Monte Carlo simulation models many possible outcomes to produce a probability distribution of results such as completion dates.

distractors.trap: Sensitivity analysis ranks which variables most affect the outcome; it does not produce a full distribution.
Reserve analysis sizes contingency and management reserves rather than simulating outcome distributions.
CPM gives a single deterministic duration, not a probability distribution of dates.

explanation: Monte Carlo yields the distribution of outcomes; sensitivity analysis identifies the most influential drivers.

prompts: Which process produces the schedule model by analyzing activity sequences, durations, resources, and constraints?

correct.why: Develop Schedule analyzes sequences, durations, resources, and constraints to create the schedule model.

distractors.trap: Define Activities identifies the work to be scheduled; it does not build the schedule model.
Sequence Activities arranges the order of activities but does not produce the full schedule model.
Control Schedule monitors and manages changes to an approved schedule, not its creation.

explanation: Develop Schedule integrates the planning outputs into a schedule model; Control Schedule comes later to monitor it.

prompts: The project is underway and the team monitors progress, updates the schedule, and manages changes to the schedule baseline. Which process is this?

correct.why: Control Schedule monitors status to update the schedule and manage changes to the schedule baseline.

distractors.trap: Develop Schedule creates the schedule model; it is a planning process, not monitoring.
Sequence Activities defines logical relationships during planning, not baseline change control.
That process performs the work broadly; managing schedule baseline changes is Control Schedule.

explanation: Creating the schedule is Develop Schedule; monitoring and managing baseline changes is Control Schedule.

prompts: Subdividing project deliverables into smaller, more manageable work packages is which technique?

correct.why: Decomposition divides scope and deliverables into smaller components such as work packages.

distractors.trap: Rolling wave decides how much detail to plan now versus later; it does not break scope into work packages.
Progressive elaboration is the broad refinement of plans over time, not the breakdown of deliverables.
Sequence Activities orders activities; it does not subdivide deliverables.

explanation: Decomposition breaks scope down; rolling wave decides the timing of planning detail. They answer different questions.

prompts: Which schedule display emphasizes logical relationships and dependencies between activities rather than dates on a timeline?

correct.why: A network diagram emphasizes the logical relationships and dependencies among activities.

distractors.trap: A Gantt chart emphasizes dates and durations on a timeline, not the logic of dependencies.
A bar chart shows activity timing against a timeline, similar to a Gantt, not the dependency logic.
A histogram shows frequency distribution of data, unrelated to activity dependencies.

explanation: Network diagrams show logic and dependencies; Gantt and bar charts show timing against a timeline.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A planner shifts activities only within their available float to keep resource use even, without changing the project finish date. Which technique is this?

correct.why: Resource smoothing adjusts activities within their float so the finish date and critical path are preserved.

distractors.trap: Leveling may move activities beyond their float and can delay the finish or change the critical path.
Crashing adds resources to shorten duration; it is not about evening out resource demand within float.
Fast tracking overlaps activities to compress the schedule, not to even resource usage within float.

explanation: Staying within float and preserving the finish date is smoothing; moving beyond float to fix over-allocation is leveling.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: A key engineer is assigned to two critical activities at once. To resolve the over-allocation the manager accepts a later finish date. Which technique was applied?

correct.why: Leveling resolves over-allocation even when doing so delays the finish date or shifts the critical path.

distractors.trap: Smoothing works only within float and would not accept a later finish date.
Fast tracking overlaps work to shorten the schedule, not to resolve a resource over-allocation.
Crashing adds resources to shorten duration, the opposite of accepting a later finish.

explanation: Accepting a later finish to fix over-allocation is the hallmark of leveling; smoothing never moves the finish date.

eco_task: Plan and manage budget and resources
prompts: Select the two techniques that are classed as resource optimization techniques.

correct.why: Leveling is a resource optimization technique that adjusts the schedule to resolve over-allocation.
Smoothing is a resource optimization technique that evens demand within available float.

distractors.trap: Crashing is a schedule compression technique, not a resource optimization technique.
Fast tracking is a schedule compression technique, not a resource optimization technique.
CPM is a schedule network analysis method, not a resource optimization technique.

explanation: Resource optimization covers leveling and smoothing; crashing and fast tracking belong to schedule compression.

prompts: Select the two techniques used to compress a schedule without reducing project scope.

correct.why: Crashing compresses the schedule by adding resources to critical activities at added cost.
Fast tracking compresses the schedule by overlapping activities normally done in sequence.

distractors.trap: Leveling balances resource demand and often lengthens the schedule, not compresses it.
Smoothing evens resource demand within float; it does not shorten the overall duration.
Rolling wave is a progressive planning technique, not a compression technique.

explanation: Schedule compression is achieved by crashing and fast tracking; the resource optimization options do not shorten duration.

prompts: Select the two statements that correctly distinguish free float from total float.

correct.text: Free float is the delay allowed before a successor early start is affected
correct.why: Free float is measured against the successor early start.

correct.text: Total float is the delay allowed before the project finish or a constraint is affected
correct.why: Total float is measured against the project finish or an imposed constraint.

distractors.text: Free float always equals total float on the critical path
distractors.trap: On the critical path both are typically zero, but in general free float and total float are not equal.

distractors.text: Total float protects the successor early start
distractors.trap: The successor early start is protected by free float, not total float.

distractors.text: Free float can never be zero
distractors.trap: Free float can be zero, for example on chained critical activities.

explanation: Free float protects the next activity start; total float protects the project end. Swapping those is the common trap.

prompts: A team documents the features and functions that the finished product must have so users will accept it. Which kind of scope are they describing?

correct.why: Product scope is the features and functions that characterise the deliverable itself.

distractors.trap: Project scope is the work performed to create the deliverable, not the deliverable's features and functions.
The scope baseline is the approved scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary used for control, not a description of product features.
Solution scope frames a business-analysis solution boundary; the stem specifically describes the product's features and functions.

explanation: Product scope = the features and functions of the result. Project scope = the work to produce it. Features and functions point to product scope.

prompts: Both gold plating and scope creep add unapproved work, but which statement correctly distinguishes them by source?

correct.text: Gold plating is extra work the team adds on its own; scope creep is uncontrolled expansion driven by stakeholder requests
correct.why: Gold plating is team-initiated extras beyond requirements; scope creep is creeping additions, often stakeholder-driven, that bypass change control.

distractors.text: Gold plating comes from stakeholders; scope creep is added by the team
distractors.trap: This reverses the pair; gold plating is the team's own additions, scope creep is typically stakeholder-driven.

distractors.text: Gold plating is approved through change control; scope creep is not
distractors.trap: Neither is properly approved; both are uncontrolled and should have gone through change control.

distractors.text: Gold plating reduces scope; scope creep increases it
distractors.trap: Both add unrequested work; neither reduces scope.

explanation: Gold plating = team-driven extras the customer never asked for. Scope creep = uncontrolled, often stakeholder-driven growth. Both are bad and both should route through integrated change control.

prompts: Select the three components that make up the scope baseline.

correct.why: The approved scope statement is one of the three elements of the scope baseline.
The WBS is part of the scope baseline.
The WBS dictionary, which details each component, completes the scope baseline.

distractors.trap: The RTM links requirements to their origins and deliverables, but it is not a component of the scope baseline.
The charter authorises the project; it is an input to scope work, not part of the scope baseline.

explanation: The scope baseline = approved project scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary. The RTM and charter are related artifacts but not part of it.

prompts: A completed deliverable is taken to the customer specifically to obtain formal sign-off that it is acceptable. Which process is being performed?

correct.why: Validate Scope is the process of formalising customer acceptance of completed deliverables.

distractors.trap: Control Scope monitors scope status and manages changes to the baseline; it does not secure formal acceptance.
Control Quality verifies deliverables meet quality requirements internally; acceptance by the customer is Validate Scope.
Closure finalises all activities; obtaining acceptance of individual deliverables during execution is Validate Scope.

explanation: Customer acceptance of completed deliverables = Validate Scope. Internal conformance checking = Control Quality. Managing the baseline against creep = Control Scope.

prompts: Which statement best contrasts Control Scope with Validate Scope?

correct.text: Control Scope guards the scope baseline against unapproved change; Validate Scope obtains formal acceptance of completed deliverables
correct.why: Control Scope is about preventing creep and managing baseline changes; Validate Scope is about customer acceptance.

distractors.text: Control Scope obtains customer acceptance; Validate Scope inspects for quality
distractors.trap: Customer acceptance is Validate Scope, and quality inspection is Control Quality; the roles are mislabelled.

distractors.text: Both are the same process performed at different times
distractors.trap: They are distinct processes with different purposes: managing change versus securing acceptance.

distractors.text: Control Scope is done by the customer; Validate Scope is done only by the team
distractors.trap: Validate Scope is the customer-facing acceptance activity; this inverts the participants.

explanation: Control Scope = monitor status and manage baseline changes (prevent creep). Validate Scope = formal stakeholder acceptance of deliverables.

prompts: What is the lowest level of the work breakdown structure, where cost and duration can be estimated and managed?

correct.why: A work package is the lowest-level WBS component for which cost and duration are estimated and managed.

distractors.trap: A control account is a higher-level management point that integrates several work elements, not the lowest level.
An activity is a scheduled action derived from a work package during Define Activities, not a WBS scope element.
A planning package sits below a control account but holds work not yet decomposed into work packages.

explanation: The work package is the lowest WBS level. Above it sits the control account; a planning package is a not-yet-detailed placeholder under a control account.

prompts: A management control point where scope, budget, actual cost, and schedule are integrated and compared to earned value is called what?

correct.why: A control account is the management control point that integrates scope, budget, cost, and schedule for performance measurement.

distractors.trap: A work package is the lowest planned scope element within a control account, not the integrating control point.
The code of accounts is the numbering scheme that labels WBS components, not the integration point.
A planning package is a placeholder for future detail below a control account, not the measurement point.

explanation: A control account integrates scope, cost, and schedule for earned value measurement. A work package is its lowest scope detail; the code of accounts only labels components.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a planning package from a work package?

correct.text: A planning package is a placeholder for work not yet decomposed; a work package contains the detailed activities ready for execution
correct.why: Planning packages hold future detail below a control account; work packages are fully detailed for execution and control.

distractors.text: A planning package is the lowest WBS level; a work package sits above the control account
distractors.trap: The work package is the lowest level, and the control account sits above the work package, not below it.

distractors.text: A planning package is detailed; a work package is a rough placeholder
distractors.trap: This reverses them; the work package is the detailed one.

distractors.text: They are synonyms used in different organisations
distractors.trap: They are distinct: one is undetailed future work, the other is execution-ready.

explanation: Planning package = known work below a control account that is not yet decomposed. Work package = detailed, execution-ready lowest WBS element.

prompts: Decomposition and progressive elaboration are often confused. Which statement captures the difference?

correct.text: Decomposition breaks scope into smaller components now; progressive elaboration adds detail over time as more is learned
correct.why: Decomposition is a structural breakdown technique; progressive elaboration is continuous refinement as information matures.

distractors.text: Decomposition refines detail over time; progressive elaboration breaks scope into components
distractors.trap: This reverses the two concepts.

distractors.text: Both describe uncontrolled growth of scope
distractors.trap: Neither is uncontrolled; that describes scope creep, not these planning techniques.

distractors.text: Decomposition applies only to agile; progressive elaboration only to predictive
distractors.trap: Both apply across approaches; the split by life cycle is incorrect.

explanation: Decomposition = subdivide scope into smaller parts (e.g., building the WBS). Progressive elaboration = increasing detail as understanding grows. Different ideas, not opposites of life cycle.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes rolling wave planning from progressive elaboration?

correct.text: Rolling wave planning is the specific technique of detailing near-term work first; progressive elaboration is the broader concept of adding detail as information becomes available
correct.why: Rolling wave is one concrete way to apply the wider principle of progressive elaboration.

distractors.text: They are interchangeable terms for the same technique
distractors.trap: Rolling wave is a specific technique; progressive elaboration is the broader concept, so they are not interchangeable.

distractors.text: Rolling wave planning is the broad concept; progressive elaboration is the narrow technique
distractors.trap: This inverts the relationship between the broad concept and the specific technique.

distractors.text: Rolling wave planning details far-future work first; progressive elaboration details near-term work first
distractors.trap: Rolling wave details near-term work first, not far-future work.

explanation: Progressive elaboration is the broad principle; rolling wave planning is a specific implementation that elaborates imminent work while leaving later work at a higher level.

prompts: An agile team argues whether a story is finished. It meets the storys specific acceptance criteria but the team has not run the regression suite or updated documentation that every increment requires. What is missing?

correct.why: The Definition of Done is the shared, team-wide completion standard every increment must meet, including cross-cutting steps like regression and documentation.

distractors.trap: Acceptance criteria are item-specific and are already satisfied; the gap is the team-wide completion standard.
Definition of Ready is entry criteria for starting work, not the standard for declaring it complete.
A quality metric specifies how an attribute is measured; the gap here is the shared completion checklist.

explanation: Acceptance criteria are per-item and were met; the Definition of Done is the broader, common completion standard (regression, docs, etc.) applied to every increment.

prompts: Which pairing correctly maps each term to whether it sets entry or completion criteria for a backlog item?

correct.text: Definition of Ready sets entry criteria to start work; Definition of Done sets completion criteria to finish it
correct.why: Ready gates the start of work; Done gates the finish.

distractors.text: Definition of Ready sets completion criteria; Definition of Done sets entry criteria
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; Ready is for starting, Done is for finishing.

distractors.text: Both set completion criteria for an increment
distractors.trap: Only Definition of Done sets completion; Definition of Ready governs entry.

distractors.text: Definition of Ready replaces acceptance criteria; Definition of Done replaces requirements
distractors.trap: Neither replaces those artifacts; they are separate entry and completion gates.

explanation: Definition of Ready = a story has enough information to be started. Definition of Done = the increment is genuinely complete. Entry versus completion.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: Three of these are requirements elicitation techniques. Which one is NOT?

correct.why: The RTM is a tool for tracking requirements through the project, not a technique for eliciting them.

distractors.trap: Brainstorming is a group idea-generation elicitation technique.
Focus groups are a facilitated elicitation technique to gather stakeholder perspectives.
Interviews are a direct elicitation technique for drawing out requirements.

explanation: Brainstorming, focus groups, and interviews elicit requirements. The requirements traceability matrix tracks requirements afterward; it is not an elicitation technique.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: A requirement states that the system must return search results within two seconds under peak load. How is this requirement classified?

correct.why: It defines a quality attribute or operating condition (performance under load), which is a nonfunctional requirement.

distractors.trap: A functional requirement states what the system must do; this states how well it must perform, which is nonfunctional.
A business requirement is the higher-level need or objective of the organisation, not a performance condition on the solution.
A transition requirement covers temporary capabilities needed to move from current to future state, not steady-state performance.

explanation: What it must do = functional. How well or under what conditions = nonfunctional. A response-time-under-load constraint is a nonfunctional requirement.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: Select the three items that are recognised classifications of requirements.

correct.why: Business requirements describe the higher-level needs of the organisation.
Stakeholder requirements describe the needs of a stakeholder or group.
Solution requirements describe features and conditions the solution must meet, and split into functional and nonfunctional.

distractors.trap: Traceability is a property tracked in the RTM, not a category of requirement.
There is no requirement class called baseline; the scope baseline is a separate concept.

explanation: Common requirement classifications include business, stakeholder, solution (functional and nonfunctional), transition, project, and quality requirements. Traceability and baseline are not requirement types.

prompts: A project manager needs a grid that links each requirement to its origin and to the deliverables and tests that satisfy it, so coverage can be confirmed. Which tool is this?

correct.why: The RTM links requirements to their sources, deliverables, and verification, confirming coverage.

distractors.trap: Requirements documentation records the requirements themselves; it does not provide the linkage grid that traces them.
The requirements management plan defines how requirements will be managed, not the trace links to deliverables and tests.
The WBS dictionary details WBS components, not the cross-references between requirements and tests.

explanation: The requirements traceability matrix maps requirements to origins, deliverables, and verification. Documentation records them; the management plan governs them.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes requirements documentation from the requirements traceability matrix?

correct.text: Requirements documentation records the requirements; the traceability matrix links them across origins, deliverables, and verification
correct.why: Documentation captures the requirements, while the matrix provides the linkage and coverage view.

distractors.text: Requirements documentation links requirements to tests; the traceability matrix records the requirement text
distractors.trap: This reverses their roles; the matrix provides the links, the documentation holds the text.

distractors.text: They are two names for the same artifact
distractors.trap: They are distinct: one is the record, the other is the cross-reference grid.

distractors.text: Requirements documentation is created during Validate Scope; the matrix during Close Project
distractors.trap: Both are produced during Collect Requirements, not those later processes.

explanation: Requirements documentation = the recorded requirements. RTM = the grid that links each requirement to its source, deliverables, and verification for coverage control.

prompts: Which statement correctly orders Collect Requirements and Define Scope?

correct.text: Collect Requirements captures and documents stakeholder needs; Define Scope then synthesises them into the detailed project and product scope description
correct.why: Requirements are gathered first, then synthesised into the scope statement during Define Scope.

distractors.text: Define Scope captures stakeholder needs; Collect Requirements writes the scope statement
distractors.trap: This reverses them; Collect Requirements gathers needs, Define Scope produces the scope statement.
The WBS is produced by Create WBS, not by either of these processes.

distractors.text: Collect Requirements occurs only after the scope baseline is approved
distractors.trap: Requirements are collected before scope is defined and baselined, not after.

explanation: Order: Collect Requirements documents needs, Define Scope turns them into the project scope statement, then Create WBS decomposes it.

prompts: Which statement best distinguishes Define Scope from Create WBS?

correct.text: Define Scope develops the detailed description of what is included; Create WBS decomposes that approved scope into hierarchical components
correct.why: Define Scope produces the scope statement; Create WBS breaks it down into deliverables and work packages.

distractors.text: Create WBS writes the scope statement; Define Scope decomposes deliverables
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; the scope statement comes from Define Scope and decomposition from Create WBS.

distractors.text: Define Scope and Create WBS both produce the requirements documentation
distractors.trap: Requirements documentation is an output of Collect Requirements, not these processes.

distractors.text: Create WBS happens before Define Scope
distractors.trap: Scope must be defined before it can be decomposed; Define Scope precedes Create WBS.

explanation: Define Scope = the detailed scope description. Create WBS = decompose that scope into work packages. Define precedes decompose.

prompts: When decomposing a WBS, the sum of the work at the lower levels must equal one hundred percent of the work of the parent, with nothing extra added. Which principle is this?

correct.why: The 100 percent rule states the WBS captures all of the work and only the work, with children rolling up exactly to the parent.

distractors.trap: Progressive elaboration is about adding detail over time, not the completeness check on a WBS level.
The 8/80 heuristic guides work-package sizing in hours; it does not state that children sum to the whole parent.
Rolling wave is a sequencing technique for detailing near-term work, not a decomposition completeness rule.

explanation: The 100 percent rule: a WBS includes all the work and only the work, and each level rolls up completely to its parent. No gold plating, no omissions.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes the WBS from the WBS dictionary?

correct.text: The WBS shows the hierarchical breakdown of scope; the WBS dictionary provides the detailed information for each component
correct.why: The WBS is the structure; the dictionary supplies details such as descriptions, owners, and acceptance criteria for each element.

distractors.text: The WBS dictionary shows the hierarchy; the WBS lists component details
distractors.trap: This reverses them; the WBS is the hierarchy, the dictionary holds the details.

distractors.text: The WBS and WBS dictionary are the same document
distractors.trap: They are distinct components of the scope baseline.

distractors.text: The WBS dictionary replaces the project scope statement
distractors.trap: The scope statement is a separate component of the scope baseline; the dictionary does not replace it.

explanation: WBS = the hierarchical decomposition. WBS dictionary = the supporting detail for each component. Both, with the scope statement, form the scope baseline.

prompts: Which pairing correctly contrasts the scope baseline with scope creep?

correct.text: The scope baseline is the approved reference for intended work; scope creep is uncontrolled expansion beyond that approved scope
correct.why: The baseline is the agreed standard; creep is unapproved growth past it.

distractors.text: The scope baseline is uncontrolled growth; scope creep is the approved reference
distractors.trap: This reverses the two; the baseline is the approved reference, not the growth.

distractors.text: Both refer to the formal change request process
distractors.trap: Neither is the change process; a change request is the controlled mechanism that would prevent creep.

distractors.text: The scope baseline only exists in agile; scope creep only in predictive
distractors.trap: Both concepts apply across delivery approaches.

explanation: Scope baseline = approved scope used for comparison. Scope creep = uncontrolled additions beyond it. Changes should go through a change request.

prompts: A developer adds an elegant extra dashboard the customer never requested because she believes it improves the product. The work was not in requirements and was not approved. What has occurred?

correct.why: Gold plating is the team adding unrequested extras beyond the agreed requirements on its own initiative.

distractors.trap: Scope creep is uncontrolled expansion typically driven by stakeholder requests; here the team itself added the extra unprompted.
Progressive elaboration refines known, approved work; this added new unrequested scope.
Adding unrequested work is not validation; nothing here confirms the deliverable meets agreed requirements.

explanation: Team-initiated, unrequested extras = gold plating. If the additions had come from stakeholder requests slipping in without control, it would be scope creep.

prompts: Over several weeks, stakeholders keep emailing small extra requests directly to developers, who quietly implement them without any change request. The baseline is never updated. What is happening?

correct.why: Uncontrolled additions slipping in from stakeholders without going through change control is scope creep.

distractors.trap: Gold plating is the team adding its own unrequested extras; here the additions come from stakeholder requests.
Integrated change control is exactly what is missing; the changes bypassed it.
Validate Scope is formal acceptance of completed deliverables, not the uncontrolled intake of new work.

explanation: Stakeholder-driven additions bypassing change control = scope creep. The fix is to route requests through integrated change control against the baseline.

prompts: The project scope statement notes that data migration from the legacy system will not be performed by this project. What is this entry an example of?

correct.why: An exclusion explicitly states what is outside the boundaries of the project or deliverable.

distractors.trap: A constraint limits how in-scope work is performed; an exclusion states what is out of scope entirely.
An assumption is something taken as true for planning; here scope is being explicitly bounded, not assumed.
A deliverable is a result the project produces; this statement says the work will not be produced.

explanation: Stating what is out of scope = exclusion. A constraint limits in-scope work; an assumption is a planning supposition.

prompts: Which statement correctly contrasts an assumption with a constraint?

correct.text: A constraint is a known limiting factor; an assumption is something treated as true for planning without full proof
correct.why: Constraints limit the work; assumptions are unverified suppositions used to proceed with planning.

distractors.text: An assumption is a known limiting factor; a constraint is something believed true without proof
distractors.trap: This reverses the definitions of assumption and constraint.

distractors.text: Both describe items explicitly placed outside project scope
distractors.trap: Items outside scope are exclusions; assumptions and constraints are different concepts.

distractors.text: A constraint applies only to schedule; an assumption only to cost
distractors.trap: Both can apply to any objective, not just one each.

explanation: Constraint = a real limiting factor (e.g., fixed deadline). Assumption = taken as true for planning until confirmed. Out-of-scope items are exclusions.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a deliverable from a milestone?

correct.text: A deliverable is a verifiable product or result; a milestone is a scheduling marker for a significant point or event
correct.why: Deliverables are produced outputs; milestones mark significant moments and have zero duration.

distractors.text: A deliverable is a zero-duration schedule marker; a milestone is a produced output
distractors.trap: This reverses them; the milestone is the zero-duration marker.

distractors.text: Both are produced outputs of the project
distractors.trap: A milestone is a marker, not a produced output like a deliverable.

distractors.text: A deliverable belongs only to agile; a milestone only to predictive
distractors.trap: Both exist across delivery approaches.

explanation: Deliverable = a tangible, verifiable result. Milestone = a significant point in the schedule with no duration.

prompts: A deliverable has passed Control Quality and conforms to its specifications, but the customer has not yet signed off on it. What is the correct term for its current state?

correct.why: A validated deliverable has been checked against requirements but has not yet received the formal customer acceptance that makes it an accepted deliverable.

distractors.trap: Acceptance requires formal customer sign-off, which has not happened yet.
Final acceptance is the overall approval of the completed project, not the status of one verified but unsigned deliverable.
Work performance data is raw execution information, not the status of a completed deliverable.

explanation: Validated (verified against requirements via Control Quality) comes before accepted (formal customer sign-off via Validate Scope). It is currently validated, not yet accepted.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes acceptance from acceptance criteria?

correct.text: Acceptance is the formal approval decision; acceptance criteria are the conditions used to judge whether approval should be given
correct.why: Acceptance is the act of approving; acceptance criteria are the measurable conditions that justify it.

distractors.text: Acceptance is the list of conditions; acceptance criteria are the approval decision
distractors.trap: This reverses them; the conditions are the criteria, the decision is acceptance.
One is a decision, the other is the set of conditions; they are not interchangeable.

distractors.text: Acceptance criteria are defined during Validate Scope; acceptance during Collect Requirements
distractors.trap: Criteria are defined earlier (Collect Requirements / Define Scope); acceptance occurs at Validate Scope, so the timing is inverted.

explanation: Acceptance criteria = the conditions to be met. Acceptance = the formal decision to approve once those conditions are satisfied.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: Which statement best distinguishes brainstorming from brainwriting?

correct.text: Brainstorming emphasises open verbal idea generation in the group; brainwriting has participants write ideas individually first before sharing
correct.why: Brainwriting front-loads silent individual writing; brainstorming is live group verbal generation.

distractors.text: Brainwriting is a live verbal group session; brainstorming is silent individual writing
distractors.trap: This reverses the two techniques.

distractors.text: Both are forms of formal stakeholder sign-off
distractors.trap: They are idea-generation elicitation techniques, not acceptance or sign-off activities.

distractors.text: Brainstorming is asynchronous and electronic; brainwriting is always in person
distractors.trap: Asynchronous electronic generation describes brain-netting, not the brainstorming versus brainwriting distinction.

explanation: Brainstorming = open verbal group generation. Brainwriting = individual written ideas first, then shared. Asynchronous electronic generation is brain-netting.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: Which statement best distinguishes mind mapping from an affinity diagram?

correct.text: Mind mapping starts from a central concept and branches outward; an affinity diagram groups many existing ideas by their natural relationships
correct.why: Mind mapping expands from one idea; affinity diagrams cluster previously generated ideas.

distractors.text: Mind mapping groups existing ideas into clusters; an affinity diagram branches outward from a central idea
distractors.trap: This reverses the two techniques.
Neither is a schedule network diagram; they are idea-organisation techniques.

distractors.text: Mind mapping is used only for risk; affinity diagrams only for procurement
distractors.trap: Both are general data-representation and elicitation techniques, not domain-restricted.

explanation: Mind mapping radiates from a central concept; affinity diagrams group a large number of generated ideas by natural relationship.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: An analyst sits with warehouse staff and watches how they actually pick and pack orders to uncover requirements they could not articulate in a meeting. Which elicitation technique is this?

correct.why: Observation captures what people do in practice, surfacing needs they may not state directly.

distractors.trap: An interview captures what people say or explain; here the analyst is watching behaviour, not asking questions.
A questionnaire collects standardised written responses, not direct observation of work in practice.
Document analysis reviews existing written sources; the analyst is observing live work, not documents.

explanation: Watching work as it is actually performed = observation (job shadowing). Interviews capture what people say; observation captures what they do.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: Which statement best distinguishes a questionnaire from an interview?

correct.text: A questionnaire collects standardised written responses from many people efficiently; an interview allows deeper probing and follow-up
correct.why: Questionnaires scale to many respondents with fixed prompts; interviews are interactive and allow follow-up.

distractors.text: A questionnaire allows interactive follow-up; an interview uses a fixed set of written prompts
distractors.trap: This reverses them; the interview allows probing, the questionnaire uses fixed prompts.

distractors.text: Both require a facilitated group workshop
distractors.trap: Neither requires a group workshop; that describes techniques like joint application design.

distractors.text: A questionnaire produces accepted deliverables; an interview produces the WBS
distractors.trap: Neither technique produces those artifacts; both are elicitation methods.

explanation: Questionnaires = efficient, standardised, many respondents. Interviews = interactive, allow probing and follow-up.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: A product team wants to classify proposed features by how strongly each one drives customer satisfaction or dissatisfaction, distinguishing must-haves from delighters. Which technique fits best?

correct.why: Kano analysis classifies features by their effect on customer satisfaction, separating basic needs from performance features and delighters.

distractors.trap: A prioritization matrix ranks options against chosen criteria but does not classify features by their satisfaction effect like Kano.
An affinity diagram groups ideas by natural relationship; it does not rank features by satisfaction impact.
Gap analysis compares current and future states; it does not classify features by customer delight.

explanation: Classifying features by their satisfaction impact (basic, performance, delighter) is Kano analysis. A prioritization matrix only ranks against weighted criteria.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a feature from a requirement?

correct.text: A feature is a capability the solution provides; a requirement is a condition or capability the solution must satisfy
correct.why: Features are provided capabilities; requirements are the conditions that must be met.

distractors.text: A feature is a condition that must be met; a requirement is a provided capability
distractors.trap: This reverses the definitions of feature and requirement.

distractors.text: A feature is a large body of work decomposed into epics
distractors.trap: That inverts the hierarchy; an epic is the larger body decomposed into features, not the reverse.

distractors.text: Feature and requirement are synonyms in PMI usage
distractors.trap: They are related but distinct: one is a delivered capability, the other a condition to satisfy.

explanation: Requirement = a condition or capability that must be satisfied. Feature = a capability the solution provides. Epics decompose into features, features into stories.

prompts: A buyer issues a document describing the work requested from a seller. The project team later writes a separate document defining the approved internal project scope in detail. The second document is the project scope statement. What is the first?

correct.why: A statement of work describes the requested work or deliverables, typically toward a seller, as distinct from the internal project scope statement.

distractors.trap: The project scope statement is the second, internal document already named in the stem, not the buyers request to the seller.
The scope baseline is the approved scope statement, WBS, and WBS dictionary, not a single requested-work document.
The RTM links requirements to deliverables and tests; it is not a description of requested work for a seller.

explanation: A statement of work describes requested work or deliverables (often for procurement). The project scope statement defines the approved project scope in detail.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes a quality requirement from acceptance criteria?

correct.text: A quality requirement defines a specific attribute or conformance the result must meet; acceptance criteria are the broader set of conditions for accepting a deliverable
correct.why: Quality requirements target specific attribute conformance; acceptance criteria are the full conditions for acceptance, which may include more than quality.

distractors.text: A quality requirement is the broad set of acceptance conditions; acceptance criteria define one quality attribute
distractors.trap: This reverses scope; acceptance criteria are the broader set, the quality requirement is the specific attribute.

distractors.text: A quality requirement is how an attribute is measured; acceptance criteria state what must be achieved
distractors.trap: How an attribute is measured describes a quality metric, not a quality requirement.
They are related but distinct: a specific attribute versus the full set of acceptance conditions.

explanation: Quality requirement = a specific attribute conformance. Acceptance criteria = the broader conditions for accepting a deliverable. A quality metric is how an attribute is measured.

prompts: Which statement correctly distinguishes the verb decompose from the noun decomposition?

correct.text: Decompose is the action of subdividing scope; decomposition is the technique or the resulting breakdown
correct.why: Decompose is the verb; decomposition is the technique or the produced breakdown structure.

distractors.text: Decompose is the produced breakdown; decomposition is the action of subdividing
distractors.trap: This reverses verb and noun.

distractors.text: Both refer to detailing work over time as understanding grows
distractors.trap: Detailing over time is progressive elaboration, not subdividing scope into parts.

distractors.text: Decompose applies to schedules; decomposition applies only to budgets
distractors.trap: Both relate to subdividing scope and deliverables, not a schedule-versus-budget split.

explanation: Decompose = the verb (to subdivide). Decomposition = the technique or its result (e.g., the WBS). Refining detail over time is progressive elaboration.

eco_task: Collect requirements and elicit stakeholder needs
prompts: Which statement best distinguishes elicitation from simple requirements gathering?

correct.text: Elicitation is broader because it includes discovering and clarifying information, not just collecting requirements stakeholders already state
correct.why: Elicitation draws out latent and unstated needs, going beyond recording what stakeholders volunteer.

distractors.text: Elicitation only records requirements that stakeholders explicitly state
distractors.trap: That describes simple gathering; elicitation also discovers and clarifies unstated needs.

distractors.text: Elicitation is the formal acceptance of deliverables
distractors.trap: Acceptance of deliverables is Validate Scope, not elicitation.

distractors.text: Elicitation is the numbering scheme for WBS components
distractors.trap: That is the code of accounts; elicitation is about drawing out information.

explanation: Elicitation draws out, discovers, and clarifies information from many sources, which is broader than collecting requirements stakeholders simply state.
